**Major city carve-outs surfaced in citation**: CA (SF 90-day unhosted cap + LA HSO), CO (Denver primary residence + license), HI (Honolulu Bill 41), IL (Chicago SHO + Cook County), LA (New Orleans 2023 ban), NJ (Jersey City + Hoboken), NV (Las Vegas + Clark County), NY (NYC LL 18), OR (Portland TLT), WA (Seattle).

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/str-regulation-check`. Twenty-two tests pin: 51-row coverage; **FL state preemption** complies without registration + uniqueness sweep; **MA 5.7% state excise** required + under-remittance violates + registration required; **VT 270-day primary residence** at 269/270 boundary + 3% surcharge required + sweep verifying VT is strictest (no state > 270); **NY 183-day residency** at 182/183 boundary; NY LL 18 registration required; DC 183-day mirror; HI Honolulu registration; CA major-city regime has no state residency but still requires registration; TX no-state-rule lenient default; unknown state errors; case-insensitive lookup; sorted all_states; non-empty citations; **FL-only StatePreemption regime sweep**; **MA + VT StateTaxAndRegistration regime sweep**.

`traderview-expense::pet_fees` is the **state pet deposit / pet rent / pet fee compliance table** — directly relevant to trader-landlords screening pet-owning applicants. Four regimes classify how each state regulates the money a landlord may charge for a tenant's pet:

| Regime                                  | States | Source                                                                |
|-----------------------------------------|--------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------|
| **SpecificPetDepositAndRentCap**        | CO     | CRS § 38-12-106 (HB 23-1068, eff. 2024-01-01) — pet deposit ≤ $300 refundable + pet rent ≤ max($35, 1.5% × monthly rent) ([Colorado General Assembly HB23-1068](http://leg.colorado.gov/bills/hb23-1068)) |
| **TotalDepositCapAbsorbsPet**           | CA, WA | Cal. Civ. Code § 1950.5 (AB 12, eff. 2024-07-01); WA RCW 59.18.260 — no SEPARATE pet deposit cap, but overall security deposit (1 month rent in CA) absorbs any pet portion ([Long Beach PM, AB 12](https://www.longbeachpropertymgmt.com/blog/ab12-maximum-security-deposit-in-california-changes-july-2024)) |
| **NoSeparatePetDepositAllowed**         | MA     | MGL c.186 § 15B — pet deposit BANNED; monthly pet rent permitted by case law ([MassLandlords](https://masslandlords.net/is-it-legal-to-charge-pet-rent-or-a-pet-deposit-for-tenants-with-animals/)) |
| **NoStateRule**                         | 47 other states + DC | Overall security deposit framework applies; no pet-specific cap |

**Federal-floor preemption (FHA + ADA)**: under 42 U.S.C. § 3604(f) and 42 U.S.C. § 12101, service animals AND emotional support animals are NOT pets — no pet deposit, no pet rent, no pet fee may be charged for an ESA / service animal in any state regardless of state regime. The module surfaces this as `federal_fha_ada_exemption_applies` and overrides the state-level caps with zero-charge enforcement.

**Colorado HB 23-1068 specifics pinned in the rule table**: pet deposit ≤ $300 refundable, pet rent ≤ max($35, 1.5% × monthly rent), breed-based insurance discrimination banned, landlord pet lien banned. As an example calculation: at $1,000 rent, 1.5% × $1,000 = $15 < $35 → cap = $35; at $5,000 rent, 1.5% × $5,000 = $75 > $35 → cap = $75 (the percentage takes over).

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/pet-fees-check`. Twenty-two tests pin: **CO pet deposit at $300 exact complies / $301 violates**; **CO pet rent flat-$35 dominates at low rent** ($1k rent → cap $35) + **percentage-of-rent dominates at high rent** ($5k rent → cap $75) + **1.5% exact boundary at $4k rent → cap = $60 exactly**; CO percentage rounds down at low rent (1.5% × $1 = 0 → cap falls back to $35 flat); CO pinned breed-insurance + pet-lien bans on the rule table; **CA no separate pet deposit cap — TotalDepositCapAbsorbsPet regime** surfaces correctly; **MA separate pet deposit banned** (any non-zero deposit violates); MA zero-deposit + monthly pet rent complies; **NoStateRule 5-state sweep** (FL/TX/NY/NJ/IL accept any amount); **federal FHA + ADA service animal / ESA preemption** — zero-charge override in CO + TX (no-rule state) + CA with state-rule states; service animal with zero charges no violation; unknown state falls back to NoStateRule; lowercase state code normalizes; **51-state coverage assertion**; all citations non-empty; CO full-violation compound path ($400 deposit + $50/mo rent at $1k rent → $100 deposit + $15 rent violations); MA zero deposit + pet rent complies; note describes CO explicit-cap path; note describes MA ban path.

`traderview-expense::eviction_record_sealing` is the **state eviction record sealing / "clean slate" compliance table** — directly relevant to trader-landlords running tenant screening reports. Since 2020 a wave of state laws either auto-seal eviction court records, restrict landlords from using them for screening, or both. Per [PolicyLink, 2024](https://www.policylink.org/resources/tools/housing-anti-displacement/eviction-records-tenant-screening-protections), 17 states + DC now have some form of sealing law; this module pins the five with the strongest current legal frameworks plus the petition states. Four regimes:

| Regime                       | States                     | Source                                                                |
|------------------------------|----------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------|
| **AutomaticSealing**         | CA, CT, NV, MD, MN         | CA Civ. Proc. § 1161.2 (AB 2819, eff. 2017); CT Public Act 23-207 (SB 998, eff. 2024-07-01); NRS 40.2545 (AB 107 of 2017 + AB 161 of 2021); MD Real Prop. § 8-401 (SB 19 of 2024); Minn. Stat. § 504B.345 (SF 3492 of 2024) |
| **TenantPetitionOnly**       | WA, OR, IL, DC             | WA RCW 59.18.367; Or. SB 282 of 2021; Ill. 735 ILCS 5/9-121; D.C. Eviction Record Sealing Authority Act of 2022 |
| **PandemicPeriodOnly**       | NJ                         | N.J. P.L. 2021 c. 188 (A 4463) — pandemic relief; no permanent law    |
| **NoStateRule**              | 41 other states            | FCRA 15 U.S.C. § 1681c 7-year limit on civil judgments applies        |

**California AB 2819 (CCP § 1161.2)** is the prototype: from the moment an unlawful detainer is FILED, the case is masked from public view for **60 days**; if the landlord wins a judgment within that window, the case becomes public, otherwise it stays sealed forever ([Bornstein Law](https://bornstein.law/california-eviction-records-sealed/)). Module models this as the 60-day window with all four outcomes eligible.

**Connecticut SB 998 (Public Act 23-207, eff. 2024-07-01)** removes a tenant's eviction record from the court system's public view within **30 days** for cases where (a) the eviction was withdrawn by the landlord, (b) the tenant won their case, or (c) the case was dismissed by the judge. The law also makes it ILLEGAL to sell the record to any third-party tenant-screening company ([WFSB News, 2023](https://www.wfsb.com/2023/07/05/new-ct-law-expands-renters-rights-erases-some-eviction-records-limits-application-fees/)). Module models the 30-day window with only the three tenant-favorable outcomes triggering auto-seal; landlord-won judgments REMAIN public.

**Minnesota SF 3492 (eff. 2024)** is the strictest: **same-day** mandatory expungement on dismissed/withdrawn cases, tenant-won cases, both-parties-agree expungement, OR three years after the eviction was ordered against the tenant ([Loebsack & Brownlee](https://loebsackbrownlee.com/new-bills-seek-to-seal-and-expunge-eviction-records-for-tenants/)). Modeled with `auto_seal_window_days: Some(0)`.

**Maryland SB 19 of 2024** auto-shields eviction records within 60 days where the case did NOT result in physical removal of the tenant within 60 days of judgment ([Maryland Matters, 2024](https://marylandmatters.org/2024/08/26/landlords-cry-foul-as-more-states-seal-eviction-records/)).

**Nevada NRS 40.2545 (AB 107 of 2017 + AB 161 of 2021)** auto-seals summary eviction records 31 days after dismissed/withdrawn/tenant-won outcomes; landlord-won judgments require petition.

**Federal floor (FCRA 15 U.S.C. § 1681c(a)(2))**: civil judgments more than 7 years old cannot appear on tenant screening consumer reports regardless of state sealing law. State sealing laws layer ON TOP of this — most push the timeline far earlier than the FCRA window.

**Screening-use restriction flag** (`screening_use_restricted_by_state`) is set for CA, CT, MD (auto-seal + screening ban) AND for all four petition states (WA, IL, OR, DC). NV and MN currently do not have an explicit screening-side ban. This distinguishes "court hides the record" (sealing) from "landlord can't use the record even if visible" (screening restriction) — different liability surfaces.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/eviction-sealing-check`. Twenty-eight tests pin: **CA 60-day auto-mask covers all outcomes** (landlord-won @ 60d eligible; @ 59d not yet); **CT 30-day dismissed/tenant-won qualifies** (day 29 not yet / day 30 exact boundary); **CT landlord-won does NOT auto-seal** + note explains "does NOT qualify"; **MN same-day expungement** on dismissed; **NV 31-day window** dismissed qualifies / landlord-won doesn't; **MD 60-day settlement qualifies**; **WA petition required — without petition no sealing, with petition eligible**; OR + IL + DC petition-only regime sweep; **NJ pandemic-only** non-pandemic case ineligible, pandemic case eligible + COVID-era note; **5-state NoStateRule sweep** (TX/FL/NY/PA/OH never eligible); NoStateRule note cites FCRA floor; **screening_use_restricted flag for all 3 auto-seal-plus-screening states** (CA/CT/MD) and all 4 petition states; unknown state fallback; lowercase state code normalizes; **51-state coverage**; non-empty citations; **AutomaticSealing regime invariant** (every AutoSeal state has window set); **non-AutoSeal regime invariant** (no other regime has window); note describes partial window progress ("15 of 30 days").

`traderview-expense::lease_termination_notice` is the **state landlord-side lease termination / non-renewal notice period compliance table** — every landlord-trader ending a month-to-month tenancy, declining renewal, or raising rent must hit statutory minimum advance notice that varies sharply (20-180 days) across states by tenancy length, intent, and just-cause regime. Four regimes:

| Regime                              | States                | Source                                                                |
|-------------------------------------|-----------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------|
| **TieredByTenancyLength**           | NY (30/60/90 by tenancy < 1yr / 1-2yr / 2+yr); DE (60-day landlord) | N.Y. RPL § 226-c (HSTPA 2019); Del. Code tit. 25 § 5106 |
| **JustCauseAfterTwelveMonths**      | CA, OR                | Cal. Civ. Proc. § 1946.1 + Tenant Protection Act 2019 (AB 1482); Or. SB 608 of 2019 (ORS 90.427) |
| **StatewideJustCauseAlways**        | WA, NJ                | Wash. RCW 59.18.650 (Engrossed SSB 5160 of 2021); N.J.S.A. 2A:18-61.1 (Anti-Eviction Act 1974) |
| **StandardThirtyDay**               | 46 other states + DC  | Default 30-day month-to-month termination; GA 60-day landlord variant (§ 44-7-7) |

**NY HSTPA RPL § 226-c (2019)** is the prototype tiered framework: landlords must give **30 days' notice** if the tenant has occupied for less than 1 year, **60 days** for 1-2 years, and **90 days** for 2+ years, regardless of whether the lease is expiring or the tenancy is month-to-month. Failure to give the proper notice means the tenancy continues on the same terms until proper notice is given and the required time passes ([Dibbini Law — HSTPA Termination Notices](https://dibbinilaw.com/understanding-the-new-notice-requirements-under-hstpa-for-lease-termination-non-renewal/), [N.Y. RPL § 226-c](https://www.nysenate.gov/legislation/laws/RPP/226-C)).

**Oregon SB 608 (2019, ORS 90.427)** is the prototype just-cause-after-12-months framework: during the first year, landlord can terminate month-to-month for ANY reason with **30 days' notice**; after 12 months, termination requires one of the enumerated just-cause reasons AND **90 days' written notice** describing the qualifying reason (demolition, owner move-in, immediate family residence, substantial repairs, etc.) ([Oregon Realtors SB 608 Summary](https://oregonrealtors.org/sites/default/files/SB%20608%20.%202019%20.%20FAQs.pdf)).

**Washington RCW 59.18.650 (Engrossed SSB 5160 of 2021)** is the strictest framework: just cause required from **day 1** of tenancy. 20-day no-cause termination by landlord is now ILLEGAL with very few exceptions; tenants are protected under statewide just-cause at every tenancy length.

**Rent increase notice** is a separate axis. CA: **30 days for increases ≤ 10%, 90 days for > 10%**; OR: **90 days for ≤ 10%, 180 days for > 10%**. The module pins these separately under `rent_increase_notice_days_under_10_pct` / `_over_10_pct` and dispatches on `TerminationIntent::RentIncrease` with `rent_increase_pct_bp` (basis points; 1000 = 10.00%).

**Just-cause-required regimes set `no_cause_termination_unavailable: true`** when the tenant has been in occupancy past the statewide threshold (12 months for CA/OR, 0 months for WA/NJ) AND the intent is `NoCauseTermination` AND `has_qualifying_just_cause: false`. Landlords with a qualifying cause path must check via `JustCauseTermination` intent with `has_qualifying_just_cause: true`.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/termination-notice-check`. Twenty-nine tests pin: **NY tiered** under-1yr/30d / 1-2yr/60d / 2yr+/90d complete with 1-2yr 30d insufficient + 24mo exact-boundary uses 2yr+ tier; **CA TPA path** — under-12mo no-cause complies / over-12mo no-cause UNAVAILABLE / over-12mo just-cause with 60d complies; **OR SB 608** — under-12mo no-cause 30d complies / after-12mo no-cause UNAVAILABLE / after-12mo just-cause 90d complies; **WA day-one just-cause required** + just-cause termination complies with 20d; **NJ Anti-Eviction Act no-cause unavailable always**; **TX default-30 complies / 29d insufficient**; **DE + GA 60d landlord notice required** (sweep); **rent increase pathway**: CA under-10% 30d complies, CA over-10% 90d required, OR over-10% 180d required; rent-increase intent bypasses no-cause-unavailable (raising rent is not termination); **51-state coverage**; non-empty citations; unknown state falls back to 30-day; lowercase normalizes; **regime invariant**: JustCauseAfterTwelveMonths → threshold Some(12), StatewideJustCauseAlways → Some(0), others → None; note describes tiered path including the "30/60/90" tier shorthand; note describes the no-cause-unavailable path.

`traderview-expense::occupancy_standards` is the **federal HUD + state occupancy standards compliance table** — foundational rule for tenant screening that traps unwary landlord-traders into Fair Housing Act § 3604 familial-status discrimination claims when the limit is set too low. The federal floor is the **HUD Keating Memorandum** (1991 internal, formalized in 63 FR 70982 on 1998-12-18): a presumption that **2 persons per bedroom is reasonable**, but REBUTTABLE based on bedroom size, septic / fire code, configuration, and AGE of children. The memo explicitly prohibits using occupancy limits as a PRETEXT for excluding families with children ([HUD Exchange — Occupancy Standards (Keating Memorandum)](https://www.hudexchange.info/resource/6632/occupancy-standards-keating-memorandum-63-fr-70982/), [Fair Housing Forum primer](https://fairhousingforum.org/uncategorized/keating-memo/)).

Four regime classification:

| Regime                                       | States   | Source                                                                |
|----------------------------------------------|----------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------|
| **SqftPerOccupantFormula**                   | NY, MA   | NYC Admin Code § 27-2075 (80 sqft livable/person); Mass. 105 CMR 410 (150 sqft first occupant + 100 sqft each additional) |
| **TwoPlusOneStatutory**                      | CA       | Smith v. FEHC line of cases + CA Health & Safety Code § 17920.3 + Uniform Housing Code (120 sqft first room + 50 sqft each occupant over 2 in a bedroom) |
| **NoMoreRestrictiveThanTwoPerBedroom**       | OR       | ORS 90.262 — landlord-adopted rule CAPPED at 2 per bedroom + FHCO 2+1 guidance |
| **HudKeatingDefault**                        | 47 other states + DC | HUD Keating Memo 2-per-bedroom presumption + state sanitary code overlay (WA additionally: RCW 35A.21.314 / SB 5235 of 2021 preempts city restrictions on number of unrelated occupants) |

**California's "2+1" framework** is the strictest pro-occupant standard: **2 persons per bedroom + 1 additional in the unit**. So a 2BR allows 5; a 3BR allows 7. State formula EXCEEDS the Keating presumption in CA (the Keating 2-per-BR would only allow 4 in a 2BR), and CA explicitly overrides Keating with its own statutory 2+1 floor.

**Oregon ORS 90.262** is the strictest pro-tenant overlay: a landlord's occupancy rule **cannot be more restrictive than 2 per bedroom**. The state statutorily caps landlord discretion to set tighter limits than Keating allows.

**New York City HMC § 27-2075** uses a pure sqft formula: total occupancy = livable floor area / 80 sqft. So a 240 sqft studio allows 3 occupants by formula but is capped by the Keating 2-per-BR overlay (0 BR → 0 effective max under the model's literal Keating reading; in practice studios get 1-2 person allowance via local rule outside the federal-floor model). Plus a NYC-specific child-under-4 carveout: one child under 4 per pair of lawful adults.

**Massachusetts 105 CMR 410 State Sanitary Code** uses a hybrid sqft formula: **150 sqft for the first dwelling-unit occupant + 100 sqft for each additional** at the unit level, plus **70 sqft for the first sleeping-room occupant + 100 sqft combined for two + 50 sqft each additional** at the sleeping-room level. Below 150 sqft total → zero occupants permitted.

**Federal floor (FHA § 3604 familial-status pretext) ALWAYS applies**: even where the state-formula and Keating presumption are met, selectively enforcing the occupancy rule against families with children but NOT against adult roommate groups is a per-se Fair Housing Act violation. The module surfaces `fha_familial_status_pretext_violation` as a separate flag that overrides `proposed_within_limit` to `false` regardless of any other compliance.

**Building code rebuttal** (Keating Memo): if septic capacity, fire code, or another building code provision caps occupancy BELOW the 2-per-bedroom presumption, the landlord may invoke that as a Keating rebuttal. Module accepts this via `building_code_caps_below_keating: true` + `building_code_max_occupants`.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/occupancy-check`. Twenty-six tests pin: **NY 80-sqft-per-person formula** (800 sqft / 80 = 10 max, but Keating 2BR = 4 binds → effective 4); NY 5 occupants in 2BR/800sqft violates; **NY small-sqft binds below Keating** (240 sqft / 80 = 3 → effective 3 < Keating 4); **MA first-occupant floor then per-additional** (150 + 100×4 = 550 sqft → formula 5, Keating 4 binds); MA below 150 sqft zero occupants; **CA 2+1 explicit** (2BR → 5 max), CA 3BR → 7 max + 8 violates, CA state formula overrides Keating; **OR landlord capped at 2 per bedroom** + flag set + only-state-with-this-regime invariant; **HudKeatingDefault 2 per bedroom** for TX; WA Keating with SB 5235 / RCW 35A.21.314 citation; **building code caps below Keating** (septic cap 3 < Keating 4); **FHA familial-status pretext overrides compliance** even when within Keating + pretext violation blocks within_limit even at zero proposed; CA state familial-status pretext flag set; studio 0BR Keating zero caps at state floor; **CA studio 2+1 yields 1** (sensible 2+1 floor for studios); **51-state coverage**; non-empty citations; unknown state falls back to Keating; lowercase normalizes; **NY + MA only on SqftPerOccupantFormula** invariant; OR unique on NoMoreRestrictive invariant; note describes within-limit path; note describes exceeds-limit path.

`traderview-expense::move_in_inspection` is the **state move-in / move-out inspection requirement compliance table** — directly determines deposit-return liability. Some states impose statutory walkthrough mandates with severe penalties for landlord non-compliance; the strictest is **Washington RCW 59.18.260** which forfeits the **ENTIRE security deposit plus attorney's fees and court costs** if the landlord collects a deposit without first providing a written checklist of damages signed by both parties ([Washington RCW 59.18.260 official text](https://app.leg.wa.gov/rcw/default.aspx?cite=59.18.260)). Four regimes:

| Regime                                  | States       | Source                                                                |
|-----------------------------------------|--------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------|
| **MandatoryMoveInChecklist**            | WA, AZ, MI, KY | Wash. RCW 59.18.260 (full deposit + atty fees forfeit); Ariz. ARS § 33-1321; Mich. MCL 554.608 (7-day window for tenant to return signed); Ky. KRS 383.580(2) |
| **TenantRequestedMoveInChecklist**      | MD           | Md. Real Prop. § 8-203.1 — landlord must provide written damage list only if tenant requests within 15 days of occupancy |
| **PreMoveOutInspectionOffer**           | CA           | Cal. Civ. Code § 1950.5(f) — landlord must OFFER pre-move-out walk-through, giving tenant opportunity to cure curable deficiencies before final deduction |
| **NoStateRequirement**                  | 45 other states + DC | Common-law walk-through by agreement only; no specific statutory penalty for omission |

**Washington's penalty** (`FullDepositPlusAttorneysFees`) is uniquely severe — modeled as the only state with this `DepositForfeitPenalty` variant (pinned by `wa_unique_full_deposit_penalty_invariant`). Other MandatoryMoveInChecklist states (AZ/MI/KY) trigger the lighter `PreExistingDamageDeductionsBarred` penalty — landlord can't deduct for pre-existing damages not documented but otherwise keeps the deposit framework intact.

**Michigan's 7-day commencement-inventory window** (MCL 554.608) is unique: the tenant has 7 days from receipt of the checklist to return it signed; if the tenant fails to return, the landlord's version controls. Pinned by `mi_7_day_window_day_7_complies` + `mi_7_day_window_day_8_violates`.

**Maryland's tenant-request 15-day window** (Real Prop. § 8-203.1) is a passive-duty regime: no automatic obligation unless the tenant requests in writing within 15 days of taking occupancy. Pinned by the 4-test boundary sweep — day-15 exact complies, day-16 untimely → no duty.

**California's pre-move-out walk-through** (Civ. Code § 1950.5(f)) is structurally different — landlord doesn't owe a move-in checklist at all, but must offer the tenant a pre-move-out inspection upon proper request, listing items the landlord intends to deduct from the deposit so the tenant has a chance to remediate. Failure to offer is itself a violation regardless of whether a move-in checklist was provided.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/move-in-inspection-check`. Twenty-four tests pin: **WA checklist at commencement complies**; **WA no-checklist triggers FullDepositPlusAttorneysFees** penalty + VIOLATION note; **WA late checklist (day 1) violates**; WA pre-commencement (day −2) complies; **MI 7-day window day-7 complies / day-8 violates**; AZ at-commencement requirement; KY no-checklist triggers PreExistingDamageDeductionsBarred penalty; **MD tenant-did-not-request no duty** (compliant by default); MD tenant requested within 15 days landlord must provide; **MD day-15 exact boundary complies** + day-16 late no duty; **CA pre-move-out offer complies**; CA failure-to-offer pre-move-out violation; CA no move-in checklist required regardless (move-in flag does not save CA from pre-move-out violation); **NoStateRequirement 7-state sweep** (TX/FL/NY/PA/OH/MA/OR — compliant by default); **51-state coverage**; non-empty citations; unknown state fallback; lowercase normalizes; **WA-unique full-deposit penalty invariant** (only state with FullDepositPlusAttorneysFees); **4-state MandatoryMoveInChecklist invariant** (WA/AZ/MI/KY only); note describes WA full-penalty path; note describes MD no-request path.

`traderview-expense::renters_insurance` is the **state landlord-required renters insurance compliance table** — directly determines whether a landlord-trader's lease provision requiring tenants to carry renters insurance is enforceable, and at what coverage levels. **No US state currently prohibits the requirement entirely**; the variation is in statutory ceilings on coverage amounts the landlord may demand, restrictions on naming the landlord as an additional insured, and low-income tenant carveouts. Two regimes:

| Regime                                  | States                    | Source                                                                |
|-----------------------------------------|---------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------|
| **StatutoryCapWithLowIncomeExemption**  | OR (uniquely)             | Or. ORS 90.222 — $100,000 per-occurrence liability cap + landlord-additional-insured prohibition + low-income exemption when tenant household income ≤ 50% Area Median Income (AMI) |
| **GenerallyAllowedNoStateCap**          | 49 other states + DC      | Common-law reasonableness applies; no specific statutory cap; includes Oklahoma (Okla. Stat. tit. 41 § 113 permits — pinned against the common online misconception that OK forbids requiring it) |

**Oregon ORS 90.222** is the only state with a specific statutory framework:

- **Coverage cap**: $100,000 per occurrence OR the customary amount required by landlords for similar properties in the same rental market, whichever is greater. Pinned by `or_at_100k_cap_exact_complies` + `or_101k_coverage_exceeds_cap`.
- **Landlord cannot be named as additional insured** on the policy. Landlord MAY be named as having an interest in the policy for lapse-notice purposes only ([Oregon DFR — Renter insurance](https://dfr.oregon.gov/insure/home/Pages/renter-insurance.aspx)). Pinned by `or_additional_insured_named_violation` + `or_landlord_as_interest_only_complies`.
- **Low-income exemption**: landlord may NOT require insurance from a tenant whose household income is ≤ 50% of Area Median Income (adjusted for family size up to 5 persons). Pinned by `or_low_income_50_pct_exemption_blocks_requirement` + 49% boundary + 51% no-exemption.

**Oklahoma misconception pinned**: a widely-circulated online claim says Oklahoma "forbids landlord renters-insurance requirements" — this is INCORRECT. Okla. Stat. tit. 41 § 113 permits landlords to require renters insurance as a lease condition. The OK rule is modeled identically to other GenerallyAllowedNoStateCap states; pinned by `ok_no_state_cap_despite_renters_insurance_misconception`.

**Module invariants** (3 separately pinned):
- Only OR has the `StatutoryCapWithLowIncomeExemption` regime (`or_unique_statutory_cap_regime_invariant`).
- Only OR has `landlord_additional_insured_prohibited: true` (`only_or_has_additional_insured_prohibition`).
- Only OR has `low_income_exemption_pct_ami: Some(_)` (`only_or_has_low_income_exemption`).

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/renters-insurance-check`. Twenty-four tests pin: **OR $100k exact cap complies / $100,001 exceeds cap**; **OR additional-insured naming violation** + landlord-as-interest-only complies; **OR low-income exemption boundary** at 49% / 50% / 51% AMI (≤50% blocks requirement); **OR low-income exemption does not fire if landlord does not require** (no compliance impact when nothing required); **OR all three violations stack** (coverage + additional-insured + low-income); **CA no-state-cap accepts $500k coverage**; **TX additional-insured naming complies** (no prohibition); **NY low-income tenant no exemption** (no statutory carveout); FL $1M coverage complies; **OK misconception pinned** (GenerallyAllowedNoStateCap, compliant); **51-state coverage**; non-empty citations; **3 OR-unique invariants** (statutory cap regime / additional-insured prohibition / low-income exemption flag); unknown state falls back to no-cap; lowercase normalizes; OR compliant note describes state path; OR violation note lists each issue (exceeds cap + additional insured + AMI); general-allowed note describes no-cap.

`traderview-expense::utility_shutoff` is the **state landlord-caused utility shutoff prohibition compliance table** — directly addresses the most common "self-help eviction" tactic where landlords cut electricity, water, gas, or heat to pressure tenants out. Every US state prohibits this, but the **statutory penalties vary by orders of magnitude**: from $100/day (CA, WA) to $1k flat + one month rent (TX) to 3 months rent or actual damages whichever is higher (FL) to punitive + treble damages + criminal exposure (NY). Five regimes:

| Regime                                      | States | Source                                                                |
|---------------------------------------------|--------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------|
| **PerDayStatutoryPenalty**                  | CA, WA | Cal. Civ. Code § 789.3 ($100/day + $250 minimum + actual damages + attorney's fees); Wash. RCW 59.18.300 (up to $100/day + actual damages + prevailing-party attorney's fees and costs) |
| **FlatPlusOneMonthRentPenalty**             | TX     | Tex. Prop. Code § 92.008 ($1,000 + one month's rent + actual damages + attorney's fees + court costs) |
| **MonthlyRentMultiplePenalty**              | FL     | Fla. Stat. § 83.67 (3 months' rent OR actual damages, whichever higher, + costs + attorney's fees) |
| **PunitiveDamagesFramework**                | NY     | N.Y. RPL § 235-a + RPAPL 853 (compensatory + punitive + treble damages for unlawful eviction + criminal exposure) |
| **GeneralProhibitionStandardRemedies**      | 46 other states + DC | Self-help eviction prohibited; tenant entitled to actual damages + injunctive relief; no statute-specific enhanced penalty |

**California's per-day formula** is the most-known: $100 per day of violation, $250 minimum floor (so even a 1-day shutoff yields a $250 base penalty), plus actual damages and attorney's fees. After 10 days the per-day amount ($1,000) exceeds the floor and dominates. Pinned by `ca_10_days_yields_1000_dollar_penalty` + `ca_1_day_minimum_floor_applies_250` + `ca_3_days_above_minimum_applies_per_day` boundary ([Tobener Law CA Shutoff guide](https://www.tobenerlaw.com/shut-offs/)).

**Washington has the same $100/day rate but NO minimum floor** — a 1-day shutoff is $100, not $250. Pinned by `wa_no_minimum_floor`.

**Texas's flat penalty is duration-independent** — a 1-day shutoff and a 100-day shutoff yield the same $1,000 + 1 month rent statutory amount (separately from actual damages). Pinned by `tx_duration_does_not_change_penalty`.

**Florida picks the higher of (3 months rent) or (actual damages)** — this is uniquely beneficial to tenants with low rent but high actual damages (food spoilage, hotel costs, lost wages). Pinned by both directions: `fl_3_months_rent_when_higher_than_actual` + `fl_actual_damages_when_higher_than_rent_multiple` ([Fla. Stat. § 83.67](https://www.frls.org/blog/utilitiesprocess)).

**New York alone supports criminal exposure** plus civil compensatory + punitive + treble damages for unlawful eviction under RPL § 235-a and RPAPL 853 ([N.Y. RPL § 235-a official text](https://newyork.public.law/laws/n.y._real_property_law_section_235-a)).

**Bona-fide repair / emergency exception** is universal: every regime includes a carve-out for utility interruption required for genuine repair, construction, or emergency (water main break, gas leak, structural integrity issue). Module bars any violation when `interruption_due_to_bona_fide_repair_or_emergency: true`. Pinned by `bona_fide_repair_exception_blocks_violation_ca`.

**Module invariants** (4 separately pinned):
- CA + WA only on PerDayStatutoryPenalty (`per_day_regime_only_ca_and_wa`).
- TX only on FlatPlusOneMonthRentPenalty (`flat_plus_month_regime_only_tx`).
- FL only on MonthlyRentMultiplePenalty (`monthly_rent_multiple_regime_only_fl`).
- NY only on PunitiveDamagesFramework (`punitive_regime_only_ny`).

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/utility-shutoff-check`. Twenty-six tests pin: **CA 10 days × $100 = $1,000 penalty**; **CA 1-day minimum floor $250**; CA 3-day boundary ($300 > $250 minimum); CA attorney fees recoverable; **WA 30 days × $100 = $3,000**; WA no minimum floor (1 day = $100); **TX flat $1k + 1 month rent**; TX duration-independent flat penalty; **FL 3 months rent when higher than actual** ($6k > $500 actual); **FL actual damages when higher than rent multiple** ($10k > $3k); **NY punitive + treble + criminal all available**; NY actual damages form statutory base; **OR general-prohibition no specific penalty** (only actual damages, no attorney fees, no punitives); **bona-fide repair exception blocks violation** (CA); no-shutoff no-violation; **51-state coverage**; non-empty citations; **4 regime-uniqueness invariants** (CA+WA / TX / FL / NY); unknown state falls back to general; lowercase normalizes; **CA note describes per-day math** ("10 day", "$100/day"); TX note describes flat + rent path; **NY note describes PUNITIVE + TREBLE + criminal path**.

`traderview-expense::landlord_lien_prohibition` is the **state landlord-lien-on-tenant-property compliance check** — addresses the landlord's right to assert a possessory lien on tenant's personal property DURING tenancy as security for unpaid rent. Distinct from `abandoned_property_handling` (disposition of belongings LEFT BEHIND after vacating) and `lockout_penalties` (unlawful self-help lockouts).

**Six regimes diverge along five axes**:

| Regime | Authority | Statutory lien | Court order required | Livelihood carve-out | Conspicuous bold required |
|--------|-----------|----------------|----------------------|------------------------|----------------------------|
| `Texas` | Tex. Prop. Code § 54.041(a) + § 54.043 | **YES** | No (statutory lien is automatic) | No | **YES** for contractual lien |
| `California` | Cal. Civ. Code § 1861(a) + §§ 1861.5–1861.27 | No | **YES** | **YES** — livelihood + household necessary items | No |
| `NewYork` | NY General Obligations Law | No | **YES** | No | No |
| `Massachusetts` | UCC Article 9 (voluntary only) | No | **YES** | No | No |
| `Illinois` | 735 ILCS 5/9 et seq. | No | **YES** (judgment required first) | No | No |
| `Default` | Common law | No | **YES** | No | No |

**Texas is the strongest pro-landlord regime in this matrix.** § 54.041(a) creates a STATUTORY landlord lien that attaches automatically to nonexempt property in the residence for unpaid rent — no court order needed. § 54.043 imposes a formatting requirement on the contractual lien: must be underlined or in conspicuous bold print in the lease. Pinned by `texas_statutory_lien_exists`, `texas_contractual_lien_without_bold_print_unenforceable`, `texas_contractual_lien_in_conspicuous_bold_enforceable`, `texas_self_help_permitted_without_court_order`, and the 6-regime invariants `only_texas_has_statutory_lien_invariant`, `only_texas_does_not_require_court_order_invariant`, `only_texas_requires_conspicuous_bold_for_contractual_lien_invariant`.

**California § 1861(a) imposes the dual gate: court order AND livelihood carve-out.** Even with a court order, the lien may NOT be enforced against property necessary to the tenant's livelihood or any necessary household items. The carve-out is a non-waivable statutory protection. Pinned by `california_court_order_required`, `california_self_help_without_court_order_violation`, `california_with_court_order_no_self_help_violation`, `california_livelihood_property_carve_out_violation`, `california_livelihood_carve_out_even_with_court_order_violation`, and the 6-regime invariant `only_california_has_livelihood_carve_out_invariant`.

**Five non-Texas regimes uniformly require a court order before the landlord may take possession of tenant property.** California, New York, Massachusetts, Illinois, and Default all bar self-help possession without judicial process. Pinned by `self_help_without_court_order_violates_5_regimes_invariant` (5 regimes confirmed violate on self-help without order; Texas confirmed does NOT).

**Illinois additionally requires a JUDGMENT for unpaid rent before lien enforcement.** Unpaid rent alone does not automatically create a lien — the landlord must file a lawsuit, obtain judgment, and proceed via judgment-lien process. Pinned by `illinois_self_help_without_judgment_violation` and `illinois_self_help_with_judgment_no_violation_specific_to_il`.

**Massachusetts permits only voluntary UCC Article 9 liens for ordinary residential tenancies.** No statutory landlord's lien exists; storage liens are available to warehouse operators only.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/landlord-lien-prohibition`. Twenty-three tests pin: **TX statutory lien exists + contractual lien without bold print unenforceable + with bold print enforceable + self-help permitted without court order**; **CA court order required + self-help without court order violation + with court order no violation + livelihood property carve-out violation + livelihood carve-out even with court order**; **NY no statutory lien + self-help violation**; **MA no statutory lien (UCC Article 9 only)**; **IL judgment required first + self-help without judgment violation + with judgment compliant**; **Default court order required**; **only-Texas-has-statutory-lien 6-regime invariant**; **only-California-has-livelihood-carve-out 6-regime invariant**; **only-Texas-requires-conspicuous-bold 6-regime invariant**; **only-Texas-does-not-require-court-order 6-regime invariant**; **self-help without court order violates 5 regimes invariant** (TX excluded); **citation pins authority per regime**; **sibling-module note across all 6 regimes** (UX-text regression for abandoned_property_handling + DURING tenancy scope).

`traderview-expense::abandoned_property_handling` is the **state abandoned-tenant-personal-property landlord compliance check** — addresses WHAT the landlord must do with BELONGINGS the tenant left behind after vacating. Distinct from `tenant_abandonment` (which addresses when the tenant has vacated and the landlord may declare the unit abandoned); this module handles the BELONGINGS leftover. Three states ship detailed statutory procedures with materially different timeline + value thresholds + sale duties.

**Four regimes**:

| Regime | Statute | Min waiting period | Value threshold | Required delivery | Disposition |
|--------|---------|---------------------|-------------------|---------------------|--------------|
| `California` | Cal. Civ. Code §§ 1980-1991 | **15 days** personal delivery / **18 days** first-class mail | **$700** | Personal OR first-class mail | Under $700 keep/dispose; $700+ MUST public auction (proceeds to county treasury) |
| `Texas` | Tex. Prop. Code §§ 54.044 + 92.0081 | **30 days** before sale | $0 (no value gate) | **BOTH** first-class AND certified mail return-receipt | Public auction permitted |
| `Washington` | RCW 59.18.310 | **45 days** if value ≥ $250 OR includes keepsakes; **7 days** if value < $250 + no keepsakes | **$250** | Written notice | Keep/dispose under $250 (no keepsakes); auction at or above $250 |
| `Default` | — | 30 days (common-law proxy) | $0 | Reasonable | Keep/dispose |

**Three regression-critical distinguishers**:
- **CA's $700 auction threshold** is unique — TX has none; WA uses $250.
- **TX's dual-method delivery** (BOTH first-class AND certified return-receipt) is unique — CA accepts either; WA accepts written notice generally.
- **WA's keepsake carve-out** (personal papers + family pictures + keepsakes always require 45-day rule regardless of value) is unique — CA and TX have no such category.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/abandoned-property-handling`. Twenty-five tests pin: **CA personal-delivery 15 days under $700 → keep/dispose** + § 1988 citation; **CA first-class-mail 18 days at/above $700 → public auction** (regression — must auction over threshold); **CA at $700 boundary must auction** (≥ strict); **CA at $699.99 under threshold keep/dispose**; **CA 14 days personal-delivery insufficient**; **CA 17 days first-class insufficient (18 required)** + note regression; **CA no notice blocks** + § 1983 citation; **TX 30-day dual-method satisfied** + § 92.0081 citation; **TX only first-class insufficient delivery** + "CERTIFIED" citation regression (regression-critical — dual-method required); **TX only certified insufficient delivery**; **TX 29 days insufficient**; **WA 45-day above $250 threshold** + auction route; **WA 7-day under $250 no keepsakes** + keep/dispose route; **WA under-$250-with-keepsakes-requires-45-days** (regression — keepsake carve-out forces 45-day rule); **WA at $250 boundary uses 45-day** (≥ strict); **WA above $250 with keepsakes 45-day**; **WA no notice blocks**; **default 30 days compliant**; **default no notice blocks**; **state routing CA/TX/WA/Default**; **case-insensitive routing**; **only-CA-has-$700-auction-threshold** (single-test 3-regime invariant); **only-WA-has-keepsake-carve-out** (single-test 2-regime invariant — same low-value-with-keepsakes input only triggers WA 45-day requirement); **citations pin authorities**; **CA personal-delivery 15 < first-class-mail 18** boundary (same property + different delivery methods yield different clocks).

`traderview-expense::adverse_action_notice` is the **tenant adverse action notice compliance table (federal FCRA + state add-ons)** — when a landlord denies an applicant, requires a higher deposit, requires a cosigner, or otherwise takes unfavorable action based on a consumer report (credit, criminal, eviction history), **FCRA § 615 (15 U.S.C. § 1681m)** requires a written notice with specific federally-mandated elements. Three states (CA, WA, NY) layer additional state-specific content requirements on top. Two regimes:

| Regime                       | States                  | Source                                                                |
|------------------------------|-------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------|
| **StateAddsRequirements**    | CA, WA, NY              | Cal. Civ. Code § 1785.20.5 + § 1786 ICRA (specific reason + but-for + 12-point font); Wash. RCW 59.18.257 + RCW 19.182.110 (specific reason + but-for analysis); N.Y. GBL § 380-b (written notice with stated reasons + tenant screening report copy delivery) |
| **FederalFcraOnly**          | 47 other states + DC    | FCRA § 615 (15 U.S.C. § 1681m) floor only; no state-added content requirements ([FTC Using Consumer Reports guide](https://www.ftc.gov/business-guidance/resources/using-consumer-reports-what-landlords-need-know)) |

**FCRA § 615 required elements** (federal floor — all five must be present in the written notice):

1. Statement that adverse action was taken
2. **Name, address, and telephone number** of the consumer reporting agency (CRA) that supplied the report
3. Statement that the **CRA did NOT make the adverse decision** and cannot explain why
4. Notice of applicant's right to obtain a **FREE copy of the report within 60 days** from the CRA
5. Notice of applicant's right to **dispute inaccurate information** with the CRA

**What constitutes "adverse action"** triggering the notice — the model includes 5 explicit AdverseActionType variants: `DenialOfApplication`, `HigherDeposit`, `HigherRent`, `CosignerRequired`, `ConditionalShorterTerm`, plus `None`. The notice obligation triggers only when (a) the type is not None AND (b) `consumer_report_contributed_to_decision: true`. Adverse action taken without consumer report contribution (e.g., insufficient income on application alone) does NOT trigger FCRA § 615 ([CFPB on tenant screening report denials](https://www.consumerfinance.gov/ask-cfpb/what-should-i-do-if-my-rental-application-is-denied-because-of-a-tenant-screening-report-en-2105/)).

**California's three additional requirements** are the strictest in the country: (1) the notice must specify the SPECIFIC reason for the adverse action (not just "based on consumer report"); (2) but-for reason analysis required; (3) credit check disclosure must be in 12-point font separately. Pinned by `only_ca_has_formatting_requirements` invariant.

**Washington requires specific reason + but-for analysis** but NOT formatting standards — distinguished from California in `wa_missing_formatting_does_not_violate`.

**New York requires specific reason ONLY** — does not impose but-for analysis or formatting requirements — pinned by `ny_specific_reason_required_but_for_not` (which separately verifies that missing but-for in NY still complies but missing specific-reason violates).

**Module invariants** (2 separately pinned):
- StateAddsRequirements regime contains only CA + WA + NY (`state_adds_regime_only_3_states`).
- Only CA has formatting standards (`only_ca_has_formatting_requirements`).

**No fixed timing pin**: FCRA § 615 specifies only "reasonable time" — module does NOT invent a specific day count. Industry best practice is 5 business days from decision.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/adverse-action-check`. Twenty-one tests pin: **TX FCRA-only minimum satisfies** (no state additions); TX missing CRA contact violates; TX missing 60-day-free-copy right violates; **CA FCRA-only minimum violates state additions** (3 state-specific elements missing flagged separately); CA fully compliant passes; **CA HigherDeposit type triggers notice requirement**; **WA missing but-for violates**; **WA missing formatting does NOT violate** (not a WA requirement — distinguishes from CA); **NY specific reason required but but-for not** (missing but-for complies in NY; missing specific-reason violates); **None adverse action type no notice required**; **adverse action without consumer report contribution no FCRA trigger**; **51-state coverage**; non-empty citations; **StateAddsRequirements regime only 3 states** invariant; **only CA has formatting requirements** invariant; unknown state falls back to FCRA-only; lowercase normalizes; **CosignerRequired triggers notice**; HigherRent triggers notice; note compliant includes regime label; note violation lists missing elements.

`traderview-expense::tenant_topa` is the **state Tenant Opportunity to Purchase Act (TOPA) / right of first refusal on landlord sale compliance table** — directly affects every landlord-trader's ability to sell a rental property. In jurisdictions with TOPA, the seller must give the tenant first opportunity to match a third-party offer, and the tenant has a statutory window to exercise the right. Recent legislative wave (2020-2024). Four regimes:

| Regime                          | States | Source                                                                |
|---------------------------------|--------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------|
| **AllSalesGeneralTopa**         | DC     | D.C. Code § 42-3404.02 et seq. (TOPA, 1980); § 42-3404.08 right of first refusal — ALL residential sales, ANY unit count, 15-day exercise window; foreclosure/tax/bankruptcy EXEMPT from "sale" definition ([D.C. Council Code § 42-3404.02](https://code.dccouncil.gov/us/dc/council/code/sections/42-3404.02), [DC OTA TOPA page](https://ota.dc.gov/page/tenant-opportunity-purchase-act-topa)) |
| **NarrowResidentialTopa**       | MD     | Md. HB 693 of 2024 — Renter's Rights and Stabilization Act; 3-or-fewer dwelling units ([Ballard Spahr — DC/MD Multifamily Update 2024](https://www.ballardspahr.com/insights/alerts-and-articles/2024/05/the-latest-rent-control-news-new-md-state-housing-laws-eviction-challenges)) |
| **ForeclosureOnlyPriority**     | CA     | Cal. SB 1079 of 2020 (eff. 2021-01-01 through 2026-01-01); 1-4 unit SFR; post-foreclosure priority for eligible tenant buyers, prospective owner-occupants, community nonprofits — 15-day same-bid window + 45-day exceeding-bid window ([Cal. Mortgage Assn. on SB 1079](https://www.californiamortgageassociation.org/news-articles/article/senate-bill-1079-californias-new-foreclosure-and-post-foreclosure-process/)) |
| **NoStateTopa**                 | 47 other states + DC subtracted (already counted) | No statewide TOPA; local ordinances may apply (Boston / Somerville pending; MA H.1260 / S.786 statewide opt-in pending; MN Twin Cities considering) |

**DC TOPA — the prototype**: D.C. Code § 42-3404.02 (1980, oldest in country) is uniquely aggressive — applies to ALL residential rental sales regardless of unit count or building type. Under § 42-3404.08, the tenant or tenant organization has 15 days after receiving a valid third-party sales contract to exercise the right of first refusal at the same price and terms. The § 42-3404.02 definition of "sell" / "sale" explicitly EXEMPTS foreclosure sale, tax sale, and bankruptcy sale — so a landlord facing financial distress can still complete a forced sale without triggering TOPA. Pinned by `dc_voluntary_sale_creates_tenant_rofr` + `dc_large_apartment_complex_still_covered` (200-unit building covered) + 3 exemption tests + 15-day boundary tests (day 15 within / day 16 expired) + tenant-exercised stops further exercisability.

**Maryland HB 693 of 2024**: 3-or-fewer unit residential properties only. Concept-similar to DC TOPA (often called "Maryland TOPA") but far narrower scope — excludes apartment buildings, focuses on single-family-through-triplex stock. Pinned by `md_3_unit_property_covered` (boundary), `md_4_unit_property_not_covered` (boundary), `md_single_family_covered`.

**California SB 1079 — distinct framework, NOT true TOPA**: applies ONLY to FORECLOSURE sales of 1-4 unit single-family residences. Tenants, prospective owner-occupants, and community nonprofits can submit a bid matching the highest bidder's price within **15 days** after the initial trustee sale, or a bid EXCEEDING the highest bidder's price within **45 days**. SB 1079 sunsets January 1, 2026 unless extended. Pinned by `ca_voluntary_sale_not_covered` (key regression target — CA does NOT have voluntary-sale TOPA) + `ca_foreclosure_1_unit_covered` + `ca_foreclosure_4_unit_covered` + `ca_foreclosure_5_unit_not_covered` (boundary).

**Module invariants** (3 separately pinned):
- DC only on AllSalesGeneralTopa regime (`only_dc_uses_all_sales_general_topa`)
- MD only on NarrowResidentialTopa regime (`only_md_uses_narrow_residential_topa`)
- CA only on ForeclosureOnlyPriority regime (`only_ca_uses_foreclosure_only_priority`)

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/topa-check`. Twenty-nine tests pin: **DC voluntary sale creates ROFR** + 200-unit building still covered; **DC foreclosure exempt** + tax sale exempt + bankruptcy sale exempt (3 sale-type exemptions individually); **DC 15-day window** day-15 within / day-16 expired; tenant-already-exercised no longer exercisable; **MD 3-unit covered / 4-unit not covered** (boundary); MD single-family covered; **CA foreclosure 4-unit covered / 5-unit not covered / 1-unit covered**; **CA voluntary sale NOT covered** (key regression — CA is foreclosure-only); CA tax sale not covered; **NoStateTopa 7-state sweep** (TX/FL/NY/WA/MA/OR/GA never create ROFR); MA citation mentions H.1260 pending; **51-state coverage**; non-empty citations; **3 regime-uniqueness invariants** (DC / MD / CA); unknown state falls back to NoStateTopa; lowercase normalizes; **DC note describes AllSalesGeneralTopa path** with foreclosure-exempt callout; MD note describes ≤3 unit cap; CA note describes 15-day + 45-day priority window; expired window described in DC note ("EXPIRED").

`traderview-expense::lease_auto_renewal` is the **state lease automatic renewal / evergreen-clause disclosure compliance table** — when a residential lease contains an auto-renewal clause that requires either party to give specific notice to prevent renewal, **four states statutorily require the LANDLORD to send a written reminder notice before the tenant's non-renewal-notice deadline**. Without that reminder, the auto-renewal clause is **UNENFORCEABLE** — the lease simply expires at the end of the original term. Three regimes:

| Regime                                          | States      | Source                                                                |
|-------------------------------------------------|-------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------|
| **PreNonrenewalNotificationFifteenToThirtyDays** | FL, WI, NY  | Fla. Stat. § 83.575 (15-30 days + fees/penalties listing required); Wis. Stat. § 704.15 (15-30 days); N.Y. GBL § 5-905 (15-30 days, must "call attention" to the clause) |
| **PreCancellationDeadlineThirtyToSixtyDays**     | IL          | 815 ILCS 601/10 Automatic Contract Renewal Act — 30-60 days for 12-month+ contracts with renewal > 1 month; clause must be "clear and conspicuous" in the original contract |
| **NoStateDisclosureRequirement**                | 46 other states + DC | Auto-renewal clauses generally enforceable as written; common-law unconscionability may apply in extreme cases |

**Florida's unique fees-listing requirement** is the strictest content rule: in addition to the 15-30 day window, the reminder notice MUST list "all fees, penalties, and other charges applicable to the tenant" upon renewal under Fla. Stat. § 83.575. Missing the fees enumeration alone voids the auto-renewal regardless of timing compliance. Pinned by `only_fl_requires_fees_listing` invariant + `fl_notice_missing_fees_listing_violates` + `wi_does_not_require_fees_listing` (regression target — distinguishes WI from FL on same window).

**Illinois's clear-and-conspicuous clause requirement** is the unique 2-prong test: the original auto-renewal clause itself must be drafted in clear-and-conspicuous form (boldface, separately initialed, etc.) AND the 30-60 day reminder must be sent. Both required. Pinned by `only_il_requires_clear_conspicuous_clause` invariant + `il_clause_not_clear_and_conspicuous_violates`.

**Boundary tests pinned for all 4 regulated states**:
- FL day-14 outside / day-15 boundary / day-30 boundary / day-31 outside
- WI 15-30 day window similar
- NY 15-30 day window similar  
- IL day-29 outside / day-30 boundary / day-60 boundary / day-61 outside

**Module invariants** (4 separately pinned):
- FL + WI + NY only on PreNonrenewalNotificationFifteenToThirtyDays (`fl_wi_ny_share_15_30_day_regime`)
- IL unique on PreCancellationDeadlineThirtyToSixtyDays (`il_unique_30_60_day_regime`)
- FL only with fees-listing requirement (`only_fl_requires_fees_listing`)
- IL only with clear-and-conspicuous clause requirement (`only_il_requires_clear_conspicuous_clause`)

Sources: [Fla. Stat. § 83.575 (FindLaw)](https://codes.findlaw.com/fl/title-vi-civil-practice-and-procedure/fl-st-sect-83-575/), [Wis. Stat. § 704.15 / Tenant Resource Center](https://www.tenantresourcecenter.org/notice_when_ending_a_tenancy), [815 ILCS 601/10 (FindLaw)](https://codes.findlaw.com/il/chapter-815-business-transactions/il-st-sect-815-601-10/), [N.Y. GBL § 5-903 (newyork.public.law)](https://newyork.public.law/laws/n.y._general_obligations_law_section_5-903).

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/auto-renewal-check`. Twenty-nine tests pin: **FL compliant with 20-day notice + fees listed**; **FL 14-day before-window violates / 15-day exact boundary complies / 30-day exact boundary complies / 31-day after-window violates** (4 boundary pins); FL missing fees listing violates (regression against WI); **FL no notice at all unenforceable** with UNENFORCEABLE note; **WI compliant + WI does NOT require fees listing** (regression target); **NY 15-30 day window complies / NY outside-window violates**; **IL 30-60 day window with 45-day complies / 29-day below minimum violates / 30-day exact / 60-day exact / 61-day above max violates** (4 boundary pins); **IL clause not clear-and-conspicuous violates** (state-specific); **NoState 6-state sweep** (TX/CA/MA/OR/DC/OH always enforceable); **no auto-renewal clause → no disclosure required**; **51-state coverage**; non-empty citations; **4 invariants** (FL+WI+NY share regime / IL unique regime / FL unique fees-listing / IL unique clear-conspicuous); unknown state falls back; lowercase normalizes; FL violation note lists fees issue; enforceable note describes 15-30 day window satisfaction.

`traderview-expense::lease_translation` is the **state mandatory lease translation requirement compliance table** — when a landlord-trader leases to a non-English-speaking tenant, California's Civ. Code § 1632 is uniquely strict in the country and grants the tenant a CONTRACT RESCISSION RIGHT if the translation rule is violated. Three regimes:

| Regime                                          | States | Source                                                                |
|-------------------------------------------------|--------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------|
| **MandatoryTranslationFiveLanguages**           | CA     | Cal. Civ. Code § 1632 — residential lease > 1 month negotiated PRIMARILY in **Spanish, Chinese, Tagalog, Vietnamese, or Korean** requires complete accurate translation BEFORE execution; failure = tenant right to rescind contract + restitution ([Cal. Civ. Code § 1632 (LegInfo)](https://leginfo.legislature.ca.gov/faces/codes_displaySection.xhtml?lawCode=CIV&sectionNum=1632.), [Bornstein Law — CA Translation Act](https://bornstein.law/california-translation-act-for-landlords/)) |
| **EnglishRequiredTranslationsNotBinding**       | FL     | Florida — English required for legal contracts; translations are courtesy copies but not binding (Fla. Stat. § 636.015 narrow exception for prepaid health / discount plan orgs only — NOT residential leases) ([Maspons — English Language Requirement in FL](https://maspons.com/the-english-language-requirement-in-florida-for-legal-documents/)) |
| **NoStateTranslationRequirement**               | 48 other states + DC | No state-level private-landlord translation mandate; federal FHA limited English proficiency (LEP) guidance applies to FEDERALLY-FUNDED housing only |

**California's exact 5-language list** is pinned as the load-bearing distinguishing feature:

1. Spanish
2. Chinese
3. Tagalog
4. Vietnamese
5. Korean

These are the only 5 languages covered by § 1632. Russian, Arabic, Hindi, Japanese, Punjabi, etc. are NOT covered. Pinned by `ca_covered_languages_exactly_5` invariant + `ca_other_foreign_language_no_requirement` (regression target) + individual sweep for each of the 5 covered languages.

**Three statutory scope tests under CA § 1632** (all must be present for the requirement to apply):

1. Lease must be **RESIDENTIAL** (not commercial)
2. Lease duration must be **MORE THAN 1 month** (excludes day-rentals, weekly rentals)
3. Negotiation must have been PRIMARILY in one of the 5 covered languages (English-only negotiations don't trigger)

Each scope test independently exits via `not required`. Pinned by `ca_non_residential_lease_outside_scope`, `ca_short_term_lease_one_month_outside_scope` + `ca_two_month_lease_within_scope`, and `ca_english_lease_no_translation_required`.

**Two CA failure modes** both trigger the tenant rescission right:

- Landlord provided NO translation at all (`ca_spanish_no_translation_provided_rescission_right`)
- Landlord provided translation AFTER lease execution (`ca_translation_after_execution_violates`) — § 1632 specifically requires translation BEFORE execution so consumer can review terms in their primary language

**Module invariants** (2 separately pinned):
- CA only on MandatoryTranslationFiveLanguages regime (`ca_unique_mandatory_translation_regime`)
- FL only on EnglishRequiredTranslationsNotBinding regime (`fl_unique_english_required_regime`)

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/lease-translation-check`. Twenty-four tests pin: **CA Spanish lease with translation complies**; **all 5 covered languages individually required** (Chinese, Tagalog, Vietnamese, Korean sweep); **CA English lease no translation required**; **CA other-foreign-language not covered** (regression — only 5 listed languages covered); **CA no-translation-provided triggers rescission right** with TENANT MAY RESCIND note; **CA translation-after-execution violates** (before-execution requirement); **CA short-term 1-month lease outside scope** + 2-month within scope (boundary); **CA non-residential lease outside scope**; **FL no translation mandate** + "translations are courtesy copies but not binding" note; **NoStateTranslationRequirement 7-state sweep** (TX/NY/IL/WA/MA/OR/DC always compliant); **51-state coverage**; non-empty citations; **2 regime-uniqueness invariants** (CA / FL); unknown state falls back; lowercase normalizes; **CA covered languages exactly 5** invariant; CA violation note describes RESCIND; CA compliant note describes § 1632 SATISFIED.

`traderview-expense::rent_receipts` is the **state rent receipt requirement compliance table** — universal for any landlord-trader collecting rent in cash. New York post-HSTPA-2019 requires receipts for EVERY payment regardless of method; four states (CA, MD, NJ, IL) mandate receipts for cash payments only; Washington requires receipts upon tenant request; most states have no statewide mandate. Failure to provide a required receipt can preclude the landlord from later denying receipt of the payment in an eviction action — a costly evidentiary handicap. Four regimes:

| Regime                                       | States      | Source                                                                |
|----------------------------------------------|-------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------|
| **MandatoryReceiptEveryPayment**             | NY          | N.Y. RPL § 235-e (HSTPA of 2019) — written receipt for EVERY rent payment regardless of method; receipt must state date, amount, period, apartment, signature/title |
| **MandatoryReceiptCashPaymentsOnly**         | CA, MD, NJ, IL | Cal. Civ. Code § 1499; Md. Real Prop. § 8-208; N.J. Truth-in-Renting Act; IL Chicago RLTO + 765 ILCS 745/8 Mobile Home Park Act — receipts only for cash payments; check/electronic/money-order payments self-document |
| **ReceiptUponTenantRequest**                 | WA          | Wash. RCW 59.18.063 — written receipt required ONLY upon tenant request, for any payment method ([WA RCW 59.18.063 official text](https://app.leg.wa.gov/rcw/default.aspx?cite=59.18.063)) |
| **NoStateReceiptRequirement**                | 45 other states + DC | Common-law and local ordinances may apply; includes MA whose ch. 186 § 15B(2)(c) receipts cover only security deposit + last month rent (tracked elsewhere), NOT monthly rent |

**NY post-HSTPA — strictest framework**: under RPL § 235-e the landlord MUST provide a written receipt for EVERY rent payment, regardless of whether the tenant paid in cash, check, electronic transfer, or money order. The receipt must state date, amount, period covered, apartment number, and signature plus title of the person receiving payment. Pinned by all 4 payment-method paths (cash / check / electronic / money order) — all triggering the receipt requirement in NY ([Rentlane — Rent Receipt Requirements by State](https://getrentlane.com/blog/rent-receipt-template-requirements.html)).

**4 cash-only states share the practical anti-fraud rationale**: cash is untraceable, so the receipt is the tenant's only proof of payment. Check, electronic transfer, and money-order payments generate their own paper trail (bank statement, transaction confirmation, money-order stub), so the landlord-receipt mandate doesn't extend to those methods. Pinned by `ca_check_payment_no_receipt_required` + `ca_electronic_payment_no_receipt_required` + `ca_money_order_no_receipt_required` — the 3 non-cash methods all comply without receipt under cash-only regime.

**WA on-request regime is uniquely tenant-driven**: the landlord has no automatic duty until the tenant SPECIFICALLY requests a receipt. Pinned by `wa_no_request_no_receipt_required` (compliant by default) + `wa_with_request_requires_receipt` (request triggers duty) + `wa_request_check_payment_also_requires_receipt` (WA covers any method when requested, unlike cash-only states).

**MA carveout pinned explicitly**: Massachusetts has receipt rules under Mass. Gen. Laws ch. 186 § 15B(2)(c) but they apply ONLY to security deposit and last month's rent transactions, NOT to monthly rent payments. Module classifies MA as `NoStateReceiptRequirement` with a citation that explains the deposit-only carveout. Pinned by `ma_monthly_rent_no_state_requirement_for_receipt` + `ma_citation_explains_deposit_carveout` ([Mass.gov AG's Guide to Landlord/Tenant Rights](https://www.mass.gov/doc/attorney-generals-guide-to-landlordtenant-rights/download)).

**Module invariants** (3 separately pinned):
- NY only on MandatoryReceiptEveryPayment regime (`only_ny_uses_every_payment_regime`)
- CA + MD + NJ + IL only (exactly 4 states) on MandatoryReceiptCashPaymentsOnly regime (`cash_only_regime_states_4`)
- WA only on ReceiptUponTenantRequest regime (`only_wa_uses_on_request_regime`)

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/rent-receipt-check`. Twenty-eight tests pin: **NY all 4 payment methods require receipt** (cash + check + electronic + money order); NY with receipt complies; **CA cash payment requires receipt** + check / electronic / money order NO receipt required (3 non-cash paths); MD + NJ + IL cash payments require receipt (3-state sweep); **WA no-request no-receipt-required** (compliant by default) + with-request requires receipt + request-check-payment also requires receipt (any method); **MA monthly rent no state requirement** with deposit-carveout citation; **NoStateReceiptRequirement 6-state sweep** (TX/FL/OR/OH/GA/VA compliant by default); **51-state coverage**; non-empty citations; **3 regime-uniqueness invariants** (NY only / CA+MD+NJ+IL count exactly 4 / WA only); unknown state falls back; lowercase normalizes; NY violation note mentions RPL § 235-e + VIOLATION; CA check-compliant note mentions paper trail; CA cash violation mentions landlord exposure for failure-of-receipt defense in eviction.

`traderview-expense::repair_and_deduct` is the **state tenant repair-and-deduct / rent escrow / habitability withholding compliance table** — universal for any landlord-trader; approximately 35 states + DC recognize some form of tenant remedy when the landlord fails the implied warranty of habitability. Mechanics vary sharply from self-help repair-and-deduct (capped 1-4 months rent) to court-ordered escrow to common-law Marini doctrine. Four regimes:

| Regime                                       | States | Source                                                                |
|----------------------------------------------|--------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------|
| **SelfHelpRepairAndDeduct**                  | 48 states (default) | Cap varies: CA 1 month + max 2/yr (Civ. Code § 1942); TX greater of $500 or 1 month (Prop. Code § 92.0561); MA up to **4 months** rent — highest in country (G.L. c. 111 § 127L + c. 239 § 8A); WA 1 month + 30-day notice (RCW 59.18.100); IL greater of $500 or 1 month (765 ILCS 705 + Chicago RLTO § 5-12-110) |
| **CourtOrderedRentEscrowOnly**               | MD     | Md. Real Prop. § 8-211 — NO self-help; tenant must petition District Court for rent escrow; court orders escrow ONLY on "substantial-and-serious-threat" finding ([Md. People's Law Library — Rent Escrow](https://www.peoples-law.org/rent-escrow-when-landlord-fails-make-repairs)) |
| **CommonLawMariniDoctrine**                  | NJ     | Marini v. Ireland, 56 N.J. 130 (1970) — common-law habitability defense; withheld rent MUST be deposited with court at eviction trial or defense is WAIVED ([Centanni Law on Marini doctrine](https://centannilaw.com/landlord-tenant-eviction-defense/)) |
| **NoticeThenWithholdOrTerminate**            | FL     | Fla. Stat. § 83.56(1) — 7-day written notice of noncompliance required; tenant may then withhold rent OR terminate; no formal repair-and-deduct |

**Massachusetts's 4-month cap is uniquely high** — the only state in the country where a tenant can spend up to 4 months' worth of rent on repairs and deduct it from rent (Mass. G.L. c. 111 § 127L + c. 239 § 8A). Most other repair-and-deduct states cap at 1 month. Pinned by `ma_uniquely_has_4_month_cap` invariant (and verified no other state has > 1 month) + `ma_within_4_months_cap_compliant` ($8k under $8k cap at $2k rent) + `ma_exceeds_4_months_cap_violates`.

**Texas / Illinois "greater of $500 or 1 month rent"** — protects tenants with low rent who need to make repairs that exceed 1 month's rent but stay below $500. Module computes `effective_repair_cap_dollars = max(cap_months × monthly_rent, dollar_floor)`. Pinned by `tx_low_rent_uses_500_dollar_floor` ($300 rent → $500 effective cap) + `tx_high_rent_uses_1_month_cap` ($2k rent → $2k cap) + `tx_low_rent_exceeds_500_floor_violates` ($501 over $500 floor).

**California's "max 2 per 12 months" anti-abuse rule** — prevents tenants from using repair-and-deduct as a continuous rent reduction strategy. Cal. Civ. Code § 1942(c). Pinned by `ca_first_use_within_year_ok` (1 prior use OK) + `ca_third_use_in_12_months_violates` (2 prior → 3rd is over limit).

**Maryland's no-self-help framework**: a Maryland tenant CANNOT spend their own money on repairs and deduct from rent — the only remedy is petitioning the District Court for rent escrow under Real Prop. § 8-211. The court orders rent into escrow only when the habitability violation constitutes a "substantial and serious threat" to life, health, or safety. Pinned by `md_no_court_order_no_remedy` (without escrow order = no remedy) + `md_court_order_plus_substantial_threat_remedy_available` + `md_court_order_without_substantial_threat_no_remedy` (both required).

**New Jersey's Marini doctrine deposit-at-trial requirement**: in NJ, a tenant withholding rent under Marini v. Ireland MUST deposit the FULL amount of withheld rent with the court on the day of the eviction trial. Failure to deposit WAIVES the habitability defense entirely. The landlord gets a judgment for full back rent even if the apartment was uninhabitable. Pinned by `nj_no_deposit_at_trial_defense_waived` + `nj_deposit_made_defense_preserved`.

**Florida's notice-or-nothing framework**: under Fla. Stat. § 83.56(1) the tenant has only two options after giving 7-day written notice of the landlord's noncompliance: WITHHOLD rent OR TERMINATE the rental agreement. Florida does not recognize repair-and-deduct as a separate remedy. Pinned by `fl_7_day_notice_complies` + `fl_6_day_notice_insufficient`.

**Module invariants** (4 separately pinned):
- MD only on CourtOrderedRentEscrowOnly (`only_md_uses_court_ordered_escrow_only`)
- NJ only on CommonLawMariniDoctrine (`only_nj_uses_marini_doctrine`)
- FL only on NoticeThenWithholdOrTerminate (`only_fl_uses_notice_then_withhold_or_terminate`)
- MA uniquely has 4-month cap (`ma_uniquely_has_4_month_cap`)

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/repair-deduct-check`. Twenty-eight tests pin: **CA within 1-month cap complies / exceeds violates / third use in 12 months violates / first use OK**; **TX $500 floor at low rent / $1 month cap at high rent / $501 over floor violates**; **MA 4-month cap within ($7,500 < $8k) / exceeds ($8.5k violates)**; **WA 30-day notice required / 30-day exact complies / 29-day insufficient**; **MD no court order no remedy + court order + substantial threat both required**; **NJ no deposit defense WAIVED + deposit made preserves**; **FL 7-day notice complies / 6-day insufficient**; **51-state coverage**; non-empty citations; **4 regime-uniqueness invariants** (MD / NJ / FL / MA 4-month cap); unknown state falls back to default 1-month cap; lowercase normalizes; CA exceed-cap note describes EXCEEDS CAP; NJ no-deposit note describes defense WAIVED.

`traderview-expense::cosigner_rules` is the **state cosigner / lease guarantor enforcement rules compliance table** — practical for landlord-trader operations using cosigners or guarantors on residential leases. Module answers two key questions: (1) is the cosigner liable for RENEWALS / EXTENSIONS, or only the original term? (2) does the landlord owe statutory notice to the cosigner BEFORE pursuing collection action or adverse-credit reporting? Two regimes:

| Regime                                       | States              | Source                                                                |
|----------------------------------------------|---------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------|
| **IllinoisStatutoryNoticeRequired**          | IL                  | 815 ILCS 505/2S — first-class-mail notice required to cosigner BEFORE adverse-credit reporting or collection; 15-day cure period; **$250 statutory damages + attorney's fees** on violation |
| **CommonLawSuretyRules**                     | 49 other states + DC | Continuing-vs-specific-term guaranty doctrine governs renewal liability (CA Civ. Code § 2787-2856 surety provisions; NY common law); no state-mandated pre-collection notice |

**Illinois's unique 815 ILCS 505/2S pre-collection notice** is the strictest landlord-side requirement in the country. Before reporting adverse information to a consumer reporting agency, providing information to a collection agency, or taking ANY collection action against a cosigner, the landlord MUST notify the cosigner by FIRST CLASS MAIL that: (a) the primary obligor has become delinquent or defaulted; (b) the cosigner is responsible for payment; AND (c) the cosigner has 15 days from the date the notice was sent to pay or make arrangements. Failure to comply makes the violation an "unlawful practice" + creates landlord exposure for up to $250 statutory damages + reasonable attorney's fees ([815 ILCS 505/2S statutory text](https://ilga.gov/legislation/ilcs/fulltext.asp?DocName=081505050K2S)). Pinned by `il_collection_without_notice_violates` + `il_collection_with_notice_15_days_compliant` + `il_collection_14_days_before_notice_violates_window` (boundary) + statutory-damages + attorney-fees + 15-day-window all individually pinned as invariants.

**Common-law renewal liability** — the load-bearing distinction is between two guaranty types under CommonLawSuretyRules:

| Guaranty type            | Renewal coverage                                          |
|--------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------|
| **ContinuingGuaranty**   | Covers original term + ALL renewals automatically         |
| **SpecificTermGuaranty** | Covers original term only; cosigner must RE-SIGN for each renewal |

Pinned by `continuing_guaranty_covers_renewal_automatically` (CA), `specific_term_guaranty_without_resign_no_renewal_liability` (cosigner walks at renewal), `specific_term_guaranty_with_resign_covers_renewal` (re-signing extends liability), `no_renewal_cosigner_liable_only_for_original_term`.

**State citations differentiated**: CA citation explicitly references Cal. Civ. Code § 2787-2856 (suretyship provisions); NY citation references common-law suretyship; all other common-law states share a generic citation. Pinned by `ca_citation_mentions_civ_code` + `ny_common_law_path`.

**Module invariants** (2 separately pinned):
- IL only on IllinoisStatutoryNoticeRequired regime (`only_il_uses_statutory_notice_regime`)
- IL only with statutory damages > 0 (`only_il_has_statutory_damages`)
- IL 15-day window pinned (`il_15_day_window_pinned`)
- IL $250 statutory damages pinned (`il_statutory_damages_250_dollars_pinned`)
- IL attorney fees recoverable (`il_attorney_fees_recoverable`)

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/cosigner-check`. Twenty-three tests pin: **continuing guaranty covers renewal automatically** (CA); **specific-term guaranty without re-sign no renewal liability**; specific-term guaranty with re-sign covers renewal; no-renewal cosigner liable only for original term; **IL collection without notice violates** with $250 damages; **IL collection with 15-day notice compliant**; IL 14-day before notice violates (boundary); IL no-collection no-notice-required (notice not triggered when no collection action); **IL violation note describes $250 damages exposure**; CA collection without notice no violation (no statutory requirement); NY common-law path + citation; CA citation mentions Civ. Code § 2787-2856; **51-state coverage**; non-empty citations; **2 regime-uniqueness invariants** (IL only on IllinoisStatutoryNoticeRequired + IL only with statutory damages); IL 15-day window + $250 damages + attorney fees individually pinned; unknown state falls back to common law; lowercase normalizes; continuing guaranty note describes automatic coverage; no-renewal note describes original term only.

`traderview-expense::mobile_home_park` is the **state mobile home park / manufactured housing community compliance table** — distinct trader-investor asset class with state-specific MHP statutes that displace generic landlord-tenant law. Four states have enacted comprehensive MHP-specific frameworks; the rest fall under standard residential landlord-tenant acts. Three regimes:

| Regime                                       | States | Source                                                                |
|----------------------------------------------|--------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------|
| **JustCauseWithRentCap**                     | CA, OR | Cal. Civ. Code § 798 Mobilehome Residency Law (90-day rent notice + just-cause + local rent control under § 798.17); Or. ORS Ch. 90 + SB 608 of 2019 (90-day notice + 7% + CPI capped at 10% statewide rent cap + just-cause after first year) |
| **NoticeAndJustCauseNoCap**                  | FL, WA | Fla. Stat. Ch. 723 Mobile Home Act (applies to parks of **10+ lots**; 90-day notice + just-cause + homeowner meeting right); Wash. RCW 59.20 Manufactured/Mobile Home Landlord-Tenant Act + RCW 59.20.370 (eff. 2025-05-07 rent increase caps in covered scenarios + penalties) |
| **GenericLandlordTenantLaw**                 | 47 other states + DC | No MHP-specific statute; standard residential landlord-tenant act applies (typically 30-day notice + contract-governed termination) |

**California Civil Code § 798 Mobilehome Residency Law** is the foundational pro-resident framework. Homeowners who own their mobile home and rent the lot CANNOT be evicted because their lease expired — only for nonpayment of rent or violation of park rules. Park owners must provide a 12-month or month-to-month renewal agreement on request. 90-day written notice required for rent increases. Local rent control ordinances under § 798.17 apply except for long-term leases that elect out ([CA MRL HTML version](https://mhphoa.com/mrl/html)).

**Oregon SB 608 of 2019** is uniquely strict on rent: caps annual rent increases at **7% + CPI** with an absolute maximum of **10%** in any 12-month period. 90-day notice required. Just-cause eviction after first year of occupancy. Module pins the 10% absolute cap regardless of CPI volatility ([ORS § 90.323](https://oregon.public.law/statutes/ors_90.323)).
