Distinct from sibling modules `lockout_penalties` (specific lockout statute), `quiet_enjoyment` (general common-law duty + constructive eviction), and `retaliation_windows` (timing-based retaliation presumptions). Trader-critical for landlord-investors operating in California or NYC where the affirmative-harassment statutes create direct civil-penalty exposure on top of common-law claims.

**Three regimes**:

| Regime | Authority | Penalty | Distinctive feature |
|--------|-----------|---------|---------------------|
| California | Cal. Civ. Code § 1940.2 | Up to **$2,000 per violation** | 5 enumerated prohibited acts; good-faith warning exception under § 1940.2(c) |
| NYC | NYC Admin. Code § 27-2004(a)(48) + § 27-2005(d) + § 27-2115(m) | **$1,000-$10,000 per dwelling unit per violation** | Requires both (i) prohibited act AND (ii) intent or causation to vacate; **per-unit multiplier** |
| Default | Common law | Compensatory damages only | IIED, conversion, quiet enjoyment breach, constructive eviction |

**California § 1940.2 — five prohibited acts** (all requiring purpose to influence tenant to vacate):

| Subsection | Conduct |
|------------|---------|
| § 1940.2(a)(1) | Penal Code § 484 (theft) violations |
| § 1940.2(a)(2) | Penal Code § 518 (extortion) violations |
| § 1940.2(a)(3) | Force, willful threats, or menacing conduct interfering with quiet enjoyment |
| § 1940.2(a)(4) | Significant and intentional Cal. Civ. Code § 1954 entry-rules violation |
| § 1940.2(a)(5) | Threat to disclose tenant's immigration or citizenship status |

§ 1940.2(b) civil penalty: up to $2,000 per violation, recoverable in small claims or civil court by tenant who prevails. § 1940.2(c) good-faith warning exception — oral/written warning about lease violations given in good faith and in the normal course of business is NOT harassment. Pinned by `ca_force_or_threats_engages`, `ca_immigration_threat_engages`, `ca_section_1954_entry_violation_engages`, `ca_theft_or_extortion_engages`, `ca_multiple_violations_multiply_penalty` (5 × $2K = $10K), `ca_good_faith_warning_exception`, `ca_good_faith_warning_does_not_excuse_prohibited_act`, and `ca_engagement_does_not_count_unrelated_nyc_acts` (NYC-specific act categories don't engage § 1940.2).

**NYC Admin. Code § 27-2004(a)(48) + § 27-2005(d) — Tenant Harassment Prevention Act (TYPHA, 2008, amended 2017)**. "Harassment" defined as act or omission by or on behalf of owner that (i) causes or is intended to cause tenant to vacate or waive occupancy rights, AND (ii) includes one or more enumerated acts (force/threats, service interruptions, repeated buyout offers, baseless court proceedings, false statements, etc.). § 27-2115(m) civil penalty: $1,000-$10,000 per dwelling unit per violation. **Per-unit multiplier** means landlords harassing across an entire building face geometric penalty growth — 10 units × 3 violations × $10K max = $300K. Pinned by `nyc_force_with_intent_engages`, `nyc_force_without_intent_no_engagement` (intent element required), `nyc_service_interruption_with_intent_engages`, `nyc_baseless_court_proceedings_engages`, `nyc_per_unit_per_violation_multiplier` (10 units × 3 × $10K = $300K), and `nyc_requires_intent_element_truth_table` (4-cell truth table — only act+intent engages).

**Default — common-law claims only**. No statutory civil penalty. Tenant remedies are case-law-driven (intentional infliction of emotional distress, conversion, breach of implied covenant of quiet enjoyment, constructive eviction). Compensatory damages limited to actual harm; punitive damages require extreme conduct. Pinned by `default_force_or_threats_common_law_claim` ($50K actual damages → $50K compensatory; no civil penalty) and `default_no_act_no_claim`.

**Multi-regime invariants** — `only_ca_uses_2000_per_violation_invariant` (3-regime sweep); `nyc_dwelling_unit_multiplier_only_in_nyc_invariant` (CA + Default don't scale with units); `only_ca_has_good_faith_warning_exception_invariant` (3-regime sweep); `nyc_requires_intent_element_truth_table` (4-cell sweep — act × intent); `penalty_constants_invariant` (CA $2K; NYC $1K-$10K; NYC max = 5× CA penalty).

**Sibling distinction note** — every result references `lockout_penalties`, `quiet_enjoyment`, and `retaliation_windows` and clarifies that this module covers AFFIRMATIVE HARASSMENT statutes (not lockout, not general quiet enjoyment, not timing-based retaliation). Pinned by `sibling_distinction_note_present` (UX-text regression).

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/landlord-harassment`. Twenty-six tests pin: **CA no prohibited act no engagement**; **CA force or threats engages**; **CA immigration threat engages**; **CA § 1954 entry violation engages**; **CA theft or extortion engages**; **CA multiple violations multiply penalty** (5 × $2K = $10K); **CA good-faith warning exception**; **CA good-faith warning does not excuse prohibited act**; **CA engagement does not count unrelated NYC acts**; **NYC no act no engagement**; **NYC force with intent engages**; **NYC force without intent no engagement** (intent element required); **NYC service interruption with intent engages**; **NYC baseless court proceedings engages**; **NYC per-unit per-violation multiplier** (10 × 3 × $10K = $300K); **default force or threats common-law claim** ($50K actual damages); **default no act no claim**; **only CA uses $2,000 per violation invariant** (3-regime sweep); **NYC dwelling-unit multiplier only in NYC invariant**; **only CA has good-faith warning exception invariant**; **NYC requires intent element truth table** (4-cell act × intent sweep); **penalty constants invariant** (CA $2K; NYC $1K-$10K; 5× ratio); **citation pins all subsections** (§ 1940.2 + (a)(1)-(5) + (b) + (c) + Penal Code § 484 + § 518 + § 1954 + NYC § 27-2004(a)(48) + § 27-2005(d) + § 27-2115(m) + 2008 + 2017); **sibling distinction note present** (UX-text regression for `lockout_penalties` + `quiet_enjoyment` + `retaliation_windows` + AFFIRMATIVE HARASSMENT + multiplier-effect references); **defensive negative violations clamped**; **defensive zero dwelling units floors at one for NYC**.

`traderview-expense::landlord_possession_delivery` is the **landlord-possession-delivery module** — when must a landlord deliver ACTUAL possession (not just legal right) of the leased premises to the tenant at lease commencement, and what are the tenant's remedies if a prior tenant is holding over or squatters remain? Trader-critical for landlord-investors whose new acquisitions come with unanticipated occupants. Distinct from sibling modules `holdover_tenant_damages` (prior tenant's liability for remaining past lease end), `quiet_enjoyment` (post-delivery covenant against landlord interference), `habitability_remedies` (post-delivery uninhabitable-condition remedies), and `eviction_notices` (procedures landlord must follow to evict).

**Three regimes** — the structural English-vs-American divergence with URLTA codification:

| Regime | Authority | Duty | Tenant remedies | Lease cancellation |
|--------|-----------|------|-----------------|---------------------|
| URLTA states | URLTA § 2.103 + § 4.102 | **Yes — actual possession** | **Greater of 3 months' rent OR 3× actual damages** + attorney's fees + injunctive relief | Yes |
| English Rule | Restatement (2d) of Property § 6.2 + modern majority | **Yes — actual possession** | Actual damages + rent abatement for delay period (common law) | Yes |
| American Rule | Hannan v. Dusch, 153 S.E. 824 (Va. 1930) | **No — only legal right** | Tenant must evict holdover party + recover from THAT party | No (lease remains in force) |

**URLTA § 4.102 statutory remedies — greater of 3 months' rent OR threefold actual damages**. The "whichever is greater" clause means the 3-months floor protects tenants with low actual out-of-pocket damages; the triple-actual multiplier scales penalty with actual harm when damages are large. Pinned by `urlta_3_months_rent_greater_than_triple_damages` ($1K actual × 3 = $3K vs 3 mo × $2K = $6K → $6K wins), `urlta_triple_damages_greater_than_3_months` ($5K × 3 = $15K vs $6K → $15K wins), `urlta_no_actual_damages_3_months_floor` ($0 actual → $6K floor), `urlta_no_breach_no_damages`, and `urlta_dollar_for_dollar_test_with_3_months_rent_versus_actual_damages_truth_table` (5-cell sweep).

**English Rule actual damages + rent abatement**. Common-law rule provides actual damages with rent abatement for the delay period. Module uses `monthly_rent × days / 30` (multiply-before-divide) to preserve precision. 30 days delay at $2K/month = $2,000 exact abatement. Pinned by `english_rule_failure_actual_damages_plus_abatement` ($5K actual + $2K abatement = $7K), `english_rule_no_actual_damages_abatement_only` ($2K abatement only), `english_rule_no_breach_no_damages`, and `english_rule_long_delay_large_abatement` (365 days × $2K / 30 = $2,433,333 abatement).

**American Rule shifts burden to tenant**. Landlord delivers only LEGAL possession. New tenant has standing to evict holdover prior tenant or squatter directly and recover damages from that party — NOT the landlord. Lease remains in force; rent continues to accrue. Tenant cannot cancel. Pinned by `american_rule_no_landlord_duty`, `american_rule_no_statutory_remedies`, and `american_rule_holdover_party_remedy_note` (note must reference burden-shifting to tenant for eviction proceeding).

**Multi-regime invariants** — `only_urlta_has_statutory_remedies_invariant` (3-regime sweep — only URLTA states get § 4.102 statutory remedies + attorney-fee shifting); `only_american_rule_shifts_burden_to_tenant_invariant` (only American rule sets `tenant_must_evict_prior_tenant_party = true`); `american_rule_only_regime_without_duty_invariant` (URLTA + English impose duty; American does not); `urlta_constants_invariant` (3-multiplier, 3-months floor).

**Damages calculation method enum** — `DamagesCalculationMethod::UrltaTripleActualOrThreeMonths` for URLTA; `EnglishRuleActualWithAbatement` for English rule; `NoLandlordDamages` for American rule. Pinned by 3 dedicated enum-coverage tests.

**Sibling distinction note** — every result references related modules `holdover_tenant_damages`, `quiet_enjoyment`, `habitability_remedies`, and `eviction_notices`, and clarifies that this module covers LEASE-COMMENCEMENT DELIVERY DUTY specifically. Pinned by `sibling_distinction_note_present` (UX-text regression).

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/landlord-possession-delivery`. Twenty-five tests pin: **URLTA landlord failure triggers statutory remedies**; **URLTA 3 months rent greater than triple damages**; **URLTA triple damages greater than 3 months**; **URLTA no actual damages 3 months floor**; **URLTA no breach no damages**; **English rule failure actual damages plus abatement** ($5K actual + $2K abatement = $7K); **English rule no actual damages abatement only** ($2K); **English rule no breach no damages**; **English rule long delay large abatement** (365 days × $2K / 30 = $2,433,333); **American rule no landlord duty**; **American rule no statutory remedies**; **American rule holdover party remedy note**; **URLTA uses URLTA triple actual or three months enum**; **English rule uses actual with abatement enum**; **American rule uses no landlord damages enum**; **only URLTA has statutory remedies invariant** (3-regime sweep); **only American rule shifts burden to tenant invariant** (3-regime sweep); **American rule only regime without duty invariant** (3-regime sweep); **URLTA dollar-for-dollar test truth table** (5-cell sweep over rent × damages combinations); **URLTA constants invariant** (3× multiplier, 3 months floor); **citation pins all authorities** (Restatement § 6.2 + URLTA § 2.103 + § 4.102 + Hannan v. Dusch); **sibling distinction note present** (UX-text regression for `holdover_tenant_damages` + `quiet_enjoyment` + `habitability_remedies` + `eviction_notices` + LEASE-COMMENCEMENT DELIVERY DUTY); **defensive negative rent clamped**; **defensive negative damages clamped**; **defensive negative days no abatement**.

`traderview-expense::lease_waiver_enforceability` is the **lease waiver clause enforceability module** — when may a residential or commercial lease validly waive tenant rights or landlord liability via contract language? Distinct from sibling modules `landlord_harassment` (affirmative-harassment penalties), `habitability_remedies` (post-delivery habitability obligations), `quiet_enjoyment` (general post-delivery covenant), and `plain_language_lease` (statutory clarity requirement). This module focuses on the LEASE-DRAFTED WAIVER PROVISION itself — when the contract clause attempting to extinguish a right is void as a matter of statute or public policy.

**Three regimes**:

| Regime | Authority | Scope | Lease type |
|--------|-----------|-------|------------|
| New York | N.Y. Gen. Oblig. Law § 5-321 | **Narrow** — only landlord-negligence exculpatory clauses void | Both residential AND commercial |
| California | Cal. Civ. Code § 1953 | **Broad** — voids 6 categories of tenant rights waivers | Residential only |
| Default | Common law + Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 178 | Knowing + voluntary + public policy test | Both |

**New York § 5-321 — narrow scope, broad application.** Voids any clause "exempting the lessor from liability for damages for injuries to person or property caused by or resulting from the negligence of the lessor." Applies to BOTH residential and commercial leases. INDEMNIFICATION clauses remain enforceable (Great N. Ins. Co. v. Interior Constr. Corp., 7 N.Y.3d 412 (2006)) — landlord may still seek indemnity from tenant for liability paid to injured third parties; only the clause exempting LANDLORD from liability to tenant for landlord's own negligence is void. Pinned by `ny_landlord_negligence_exculpatory_void`, `ny_negligence_exculpatory_applies_to_commercial`, `ny_jury_trial_waiver_outside_5_321_scope` (jury waiver enforceable in NY with knowing consent), `ny_jury_trial_waiver_without_consent_unenforceable`, and `ny_indemnity_distinction_note_when_5_321_engages` (UX-text regression for indemnity-vs-exculpation distinction).

**California § 1953 — broadest residential protection in the U.S.** Six categories of waivers automatically void in residential leases:

| Subsection | Voided waiver category |
|------------|-------------------------|
| § 1953(a)(1) | § 1950.5 (security deposit) or § 1954 (landlord entry) rights |
| § 1953(a)(2) | Right to assert cause of action against lessor |
| § 1953(a)(3) | Right to notice or hearing required by law |
| § 1953(a)(4) | Procedural rights in litigation (including jury trial waiver) |
| § 1953(a)(5) | Right to have lessor exercise duty of care to prevent personal injury or property damage |
| § 1953(a)(6) | Cumulative remedies under the lease |

§ 1953 applies ONLY to residential leases. Commercial waivers in California fall under common-law analysis. Pinned by `ca_residential_jury_trial_waiver_void`, `ca_residential_deposit_rights_waiver_void`, `ca_residential_future_cause_of_action_void`, `ca_residential_notice_hearing_void`, `ca_residential_habitability_void`, `ca_residential_cumulative_remedies_void`, `ca_commercial_lease_outside_1953_scope` (commercial waiver enforceable with consent), `ca_1953_applies_only_to_residential_invariant` (residential vs commercial head-to-head), and `ca_residential_all_seven_waiver_types_void` (7-prong sweep — all enum types void in CA residential).

**Default common-law analysis — knowing/voluntary + public policy**. Waiver enforceable if BOTH (a) knowing and voluntary consent (no adhesion-contract procedural unconscionability) AND (b) waiver does not violate public policy. Public-policy-protected categories (habitability, duty of care, landlord negligence exculpation) are void REGARDLESS of consent. Pinned by `default_knowing_voluntary_enforceable`, `default_not_knowing_voluntary_unenforceable`, `default_habitability_waiver_void_regardless_of_consent`, `default_negligence_exculpatory_void_regardless_of_consent`, and `default_public_policy_voids_regardless_of_consent_truth_table` (4-cell sweep — public-policy-protected category voids regardless of consent; non-protected category requires knowing consent).

**Multi-regime invariants** — `only_california_1953_engages_residential_only_protection` (3-regime sweep); `ny_5_321_applies_to_both_residential_and_commercial_invariant` (both residential and commercial coverage); `ca_1953_applies_only_to_residential_invariant` (residential vs commercial divergence). `ca_residential_all_seven_waiver_types_void` confirms all enum waiver types are void in CA residential.

**No-waiver-clause short circuit** — when `waiver_in_lease_agreement` is false, no analysis is performed; result returned immediately as compliant with explanatory note. Pinned by `no_waiver_clause_no_analysis`.

**Sibling distinction note** — every result references `landlord_harassment` (affirmative-harassment penalties), `habitability_remedies` (post-delivery habitability obligations), `quiet_enjoyment` (general post-delivery covenant), and `plain_language_lease` (statutory clarity requirement), and clarifies that this module covers LEASE-DRAFTED WAIVER PROVISIONS specifically. Pinned by `sibling_distinction_note_present` (UX-text regression).

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/lease-waiver-enforceability`. Twenty-four tests pin: **no waiver clause no analysis**; **NY landlord-negligence exculpatory void**; **NY negligence exculpatory applies to commercial**; **NY jury trial waiver outside § 5-321 scope**; **NY jury trial waiver without consent unenforceable**; **CA residential jury trial waiver void** (§ 1953(a)(4)); **CA residential deposit rights waiver void** (§ 1953(a)(1)); **CA residential future cause of action void** (§ 1953(a)(2)); **CA residential notice/hearing void** (§ 1953(a)(3)); **CA residential habitability void** (§ 1953(a)(5)); **CA residential cumulative remedies void** (§ 1953(a)(6)); **CA commercial lease outside § 1953 scope** (residential-only protection); **default knowing voluntary enforceable**; **default not knowing voluntary unenforceable**; **default habitability waiver void regardless of consent**; **default negligence exculpatory void regardless of consent**; **only California § 1953 engages residential-only protection** (3-regime sweep); **NY § 5-321 applies to both residential and commercial invariant**; **CA § 1953 applies only to residential invariant**; **CA residential all seven waiver types void**; **default public policy voids regardless of consent truth table** (4-cell sweep); **citation pins all subsections** (§ 5-321 + § 1953 + (a)(1)-(6) + § 1950.5 + § 1954 + Restatement § 178 + Great N. Ins. Co. 7 N.Y.3d 412); **sibling distinction note present** (UX-text regression for `landlord_harassment` + `habitability_remedies` + `quiet_enjoyment` + `plain_language_lease` + LEASE-DRAFTED WAIVER PROVISIONS); **NY indemnity distinction note when § 5-321 engages** (UX-text regression — indemnification clauses remain enforceable per Great N. Ins. Co.).

`traderview-expense::landlord_retaliation_damages` is the **landlord retaliation damages math module** — how much may a tenant recover when the landlord has retaliated against the tenant's exercise of statutory rights or good-faith complaint? Distinct from sibling module `retaliation_windows` (timing-based rebuttable-presumption windows); this module focuses on the DAMAGES RECOVERY math after retaliation has been established. Trader-critical for landlord-investors operating across multiple jurisdictions — state divergence ranges from common-law actual damages to multi-month rent statutory minimums plus punitive damages.

**Four regimes**:

| Regime | Authority | Damages formula | Attorney fees | Presumption window | Rebuttal standard |
|--------|-----------|-----------------|---------------|--------------------|-------------------|
| California | Cal. Civ. Code § 1942.5(h) | Actual + **$100-$2,000 punitive per act** (malice req'd) | Statutory | 180 days | Preponderance |
| Massachusetts | G.L. c. 186 § 18 | **max(1 month rent floor, actual)**; ceiling = max(3 mo, actual) | Statutory | 6 months | **Clear and convincing** |
| New Jersey | N.J.S.A. 2A:42-10.10 | Actual damages + injunctive/equitable | Statutory | ~90 days | Preponderance (case-by-case) |
| Default | Common law + Restatement (2d) of Property § 14.8 | Actual damages only | Only if lease permits | n/a | n/a |

**California § 1942.5(h) — punitive damages $100-$2,000 per retaliatory act**. When tenant shows fraud, oppression, or malice, court may award punitive damages clamped between $100 floor and $2,000 ceiling per retaliatory act, multiplied by the count of separate retaliatory acts. Without malice showing, only actual damages recoverable. Statutory attorney's fees + costs available. 180-day presumption window under § 1942.5(a). Pinned by `ca_actual_only_when_no_malice`, `ca_actual_plus_punitive_when_malice_shown` ($5K actual + 1 × $2K = $7K), `ca_multiple_retaliatory_acts_multiply_punitive` (5 acts × $2K = $10K), `ca_punitive_per_act_clamped_to_max` (over $2K → $2K), `ca_punitive_per_act_clamped_to_min` (under $100 → $100), `ca_180_day_presumption_window`, and `ca_punitive_clamped_truth_table` (5-cell sweep).

**Massachusetts § 18 — strongest rebuttal standard in the U.S.** Statutory damages = MAX(1 month's rent floor, actual damages); effective ceiling = MAX(3 months' rent, actual). 6-month presumption window. Rebuttal requires CLEAR AND CONVINCING evidence (highest civil standard; vs. preponderance in CA/NJ). Waiver of § 18 in any lease void and unenforceable. Pinned by `ma_actual_below_one_month_floored_at_one_month` (1-month floor wins on low actual), `ma_actual_above_three_months_uses_actual` (10K actual wins over 3mo cap), `ma_actual_between_floor_and_three_months_uses_actual`, `ma_clear_and_convincing_rebuttal_flagged`, `ma_6_month_presumption_window`, and `only_ma_requires_clear_and_convincing_invariant` (4-regime sweep).

**New Jersey § 2A:42-10.10 — Reprisal Law**. Actual damages + injunctive/equitable relief. Statutory presumption of retaliation when reprisal follows protected tenant activity; landlord may rebut by showing commencement INDEPENDENT of protected activity. Applies to all rental dwellings EXCEPT owner-occupied premises with ≤ 2 rental units. Pinned by `nj_actual_damages_only_plus_attorney_fees` and `nj_90_day_presumption_window`.

**Default common-law analysis**. Actual damages only. Attorney's fees recoverable only if lease expressly permits. Most states without explicit retaliation statutes follow this default. Restatement (Second) of Property § 14.8 governs jurisdiction-specific common-law retaliation analysis. Pinned by `default_actual_only_no_attorney_fees` and `default_no_presumption_window`.

**Rebuttal short-circuit** — when landlord successfully rebuts the presumption (regime-specific standard), no damages recoverable under this module's regime-specific math; common-law fallback claims (constructive eviction, IIED) remain available. Pinned by `rebutted_presumption_no_damages_all_regimes` (4-regime sweep).

**Multi-regime invariants** — `only_ca_engages_punitive_damages_invariant` (4-regime sweep — only CA generates punitive damages); `only_ma_floors_at_one_month_rent_invariant` (only MA has a statutory rent-based floor on damages); `only_ma_requires_clear_and_convincing_invariant` (only MA imposes the highest civil rebuttal standard); `statutory_attorney_fees_three_regimes_invariant` (CA + MA + NJ all provide statutory attorney fees; Default does not); `penalty_constants_invariant` (CA $100-$2,000 / 180 day; MA 1-3 months / 6 month; NJ 90 day).

**Sibling distinction note** — every result references `retaliation_windows` (timing-based presumption windows), `landlord_harassment` (affirmative-harassment civil penalty statutes), `lockout_penalties` (lockout-specific damages), `landlord_possession_delivery` (lease-commencement delivery duty + URLTA damages), and clarifies that this module covers DAMAGES RECOVERY math after retaliation finding. Pinned by `sibling_distinction_note_present` (UX-text regression).

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/landlord-retaliation-damages`. Twenty-seven tests pin: **CA actual only when no malice**; **CA actual plus punitive when malice shown** ($7K total); **CA multiple retaliatory acts multiply punitive** (5 × $2K = $10K); **CA punitive per act clamped to max** ($2K ceiling); **CA punitive per act clamped to min** ($100 floor); **CA 180-day presumption window**; **MA actual below one month floored at one month**; **MA actual above three months uses actual**; **MA actual between floor and three months uses actual**; **MA clear and convincing rebuttal flagged**; **MA 6-month presumption window**; **NJ actual damages only plus attorney fees**; **NJ 90-day presumption window**; **default actual only no attorney fees**; **default no presumption window**; **rebutted presumption no damages all regimes** (4-regime sweep); **only CA engages punitive damages invariant** (4-regime sweep); **only MA floors at one month rent invariant**; **only MA requires clear and convincing invariant**; **statutory attorney fees three regimes invariant** (CA + MA + NJ vs Default); **CA punitive clamped truth table** (5-cell sweep over input vs output); **penalty constants invariant** (CA $100-$2K / 180 day; MA 1-3 mo / 6 mo; NJ 90 day); **citation pins all subsections** (§ 1942.5(h) + § 1942.5(a) + c. 186 § 18 + § 2A:42-10.10 + § 2A:42-10.11 + Restatement § 14.8 + clear-and-convincing); **sibling distinction note present** (UX-text regression for `retaliation_windows` + `landlord_harassment` + `lockout_penalties` + `landlord_possession_delivery` + DAMAGES RECOVERY focus); **defensive negative rent clamped**; **defensive negative actual damages clamped**; **defensive negative retaliation acts clamped**.

`traderview-expense::last_month_rent_offset` is the **tenant security deposit-as-last-month's-rent offset right module** — when may a tenant unilaterally withhold last month's rent payment on grounds that the security deposit serves as security for unpaid rent? Distinct from sibling modules `security_deposit_caps` (statutory caps on deposit amount), `deposit_return_windows` (refund timelines), `damage_deduction_itemization` (deductions from deposit), and `security_deposit_bank_disclosure` (bank location disclosure including NY 6+ unit interest-bearing requirement). This module focuses on the TENANT-SIDE OFFSET RIGHT.

**Four regimes**:

| Regime | Authority | Tenant offset right | Penalty |
|--------|-----------|---------------------|---------|
| Texas | Tex. Prop. Code § 92.108 | **PROHIBITED** (with § 92.056 health/safety exception) | **TREBLE damages + attorney fees** (strongest in U.S.) |
| California | Cal. Civ. Code § 1950.5 | **LABEL-DEPENDENT** — yes if lease labels payment as "last month's rent"; no if labeled "security" | n/a (label controls) |
| New York | N.Y. Gen. Oblig. Law § 7-103 | **PROHIBITED** (landlord may apply at end; tenant may not unilaterally) | Common-law remedies |
| Default | Common-law separation | **PROHIBITED** absent express lease provision | Common-law remedies |

**Texas § 92.108 — strongest tenant-side penalty in the U.S.** Strict prohibition on tenant withholding last month's rent against security deposit. § 92.108(b) bad-faith violation triggers **TREBLE DAMAGES** (3× the rent wrongfully withheld) **plus reasonable attorney's fees** in landlord's suit to recover the rent. Single statutory exception under § 92.056: if tenant has properly terminated lease for landlord's failure to repair a health/safety condition, tenant may deduct deposit from rent owed. Pinned by `texas_attempted_offset_violation`, `texas_bad_faith_treble_damages_engages` (3 × $2K = $6K), `texas_no_bad_faith_no_treble`, `texas_92_056_repair_exception_permits_offset`, and `texas_treble_math_invariant` (4-cell sweep over rent levels with 3× ratio confirmation).

**California § 1950.5 — label-dependent treatment.** Cal. Civ. Code § 1950.5 treats advance payments as security UNLESS the lease expressly labels them as "last month's rent." If lease labels payment as "last month's rent," tenant is RELIEVED of paying that month (effectively pre-paid). If labeled as "security" or "security for last month's rent," tenant must pay last month's rent separately. AB 12 (effective July 1, 2024) capped total security deposit at 1 month's rent for most residential rentals — eliminated the traditional "first + last + security" combo for landlords. Pinned by `ca_lease_labels_as_last_month_permits_relief`, `ca_lease_labels_as_security_no_relief`, and `ca_ab12_note_present` (UX-text regression for AB 12 + July 1, 2024 effective date).

**New York GOL § 7-103 — trust-fund principle.** Deposit must be held in trust; not commingled with landlord's funds. Landlord may apply deposit to last month's rent at end of lease, but tenant may NOT unilaterally apply. Companion to § 7-103(2) 6+ unit interest-bearing requirement covered in `security_deposit_bank_disclosure` module. Pinned by `ny_attempted_offset_violation` and `ny_no_offset_no_violation`.

**Default common-law separation rule.** Security deposit and rent are separate obligations; tenant cannot unilaterally offset absent express lease provision authorizing it. Most states follow this default. Pinned by `default_attempted_offset_violation` and `default_no_offset_no_violation`.

**Multi-regime invariants** — `only_texas_engages_treble_damages_invariant` (4-regime sweep — only TX has statutory treble); `only_ca_label_dependent_invariant` (4-regime sweep — only CA grants offset relief based on lease label); `texas_treble_math_invariant` (4-cell sweep at $1K/$2K/$5K/$10K monthly rent confirms 3× multiplier); `texas_treble_multiplier_invariant` (constant = 3).

**Sibling distinction note** — every result references the four deposit-side sibling modules and clarifies that this module covers TENANT-SIDE OFFSET RIGHT specifically (not the deposit cap, refund timeline, deduction itemization, or bank disclosure). Pinned by `sibling_distinction_note_present` (UX-text regression).

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/last-month-rent-offset`. Twenty tests pin: **Texas attempted offset violation**; **Texas bad-faith treble damages engages** ($6K on $2K rent); **Texas no bad faith no treble**; **Texas § 92.056 repair exception permits offset**; **Texas no attempted offset no violation**; **CA lease labels as last month rent permits relief**; **CA lease labels as security no relief**; **CA AB 12 note present** (effective July 1, 2024); **NY attempted offset violation** (trust-fund principle); **NY no offset no violation**; **default attempted offset violation** (common-law separation); **default no offset no violation**; **only Texas engages treble damages invariant** (4-regime sweep); **only CA label-dependent invariant** (4-regime sweep); **Texas treble math invariant** (4-cell rent-level sweep with 3× ratio); **Texas treble multiplier invariant** (= 3); **citation pins all subsections** (§ 92.108 + § 92.108(b) + § 92.056 + § 1950.5 + § 1950.5(b) + § 1950.5(c) + § 7-103 + § 7-103(1)/(2) + AB 12 + July 1, 2024); **sibling distinction note present** (UX-text regression for `security_deposit_caps` + `deposit_return_windows` + `damage_deduction_itemization` + `security_deposit_bank_disclosure` + TENANT-SIDE OFFSET RIGHT); **defensive negative rent clamped**; **Texas extreme rent no overflow** ($1B × 3 = $3B via saturating math).

`traderview-expense::emotional_support_animal_documentation` is the **Emotional Support Animal (ESA) documentation requirements module** — when may a landlord rely on tenant-provided ESA documentation to grant a reasonable-accommodation request, and when may documentation be rejected as unreliable? Distinct from sibling module `service_animal` (ADA-covered trained service animals receive broader protection including ADA Title II/III public accommodation rights). ESAs receive housing-only protection under FHA reasonable accommodation. Both California and Florida enacted documentation reliability statutes in response to widespread fraudulent ESA-letter mills in 2020-2022.

**Three regimes**:

| Regime | Authority | Key requirement | Penalty for fraud |
|--------|-----------|-----------------|-------------------|
| California | Cal. Health & Safety Code § 122318 (AB 468, eff. Jan 1, 2022) | 5-element practitioner test | Misdemeanor + practitioner discipline |
| Florida | Fla. Stat. § 760.27 (eff. July 1, 2020) | Personal knowledge + telehealth FL-licensed or out-of-state in-person visit | 2nd-degree misdemeanor + practitioner discipline |
| Default | Federal FHA + HUD Notice FHEO-2020-01 | Reasonable accommodation; reliable documentation if not readily apparent | None (state-only misdemeanor) |

**California § 122318 — strictest 5-element practitioner test**. Healthcare practitioners may NOT provide ESA documentation unless ALL FIVE elements are satisfied:

| Element | Requirement |
|---------|-------------|
| (1) | Valid, active license + writes all information in document |
| (2) | Licensed in the jurisdiction |
| (3) | **≥ 30-day** prior therapeutic relationship |
| (4) | Clinical evaluation completed |
| (5) | Misdemeanor-misrepresentation warning provided (written or verbal) |

Pinned by `ca_all_5_elements_satisfied_reliable`, `ca_missing_license_unreliable`, `ca_missing_jurisdiction_unreliable`, `ca_29_day_relationship_unreliable`, `ca_exactly_30_day_relationship_reliable` (boundary at exact 30 days), `ca_missing_evaluation_unreliable`, `ca_missing_warning_unreliable`, `ca_practitioner_discipline_on_violation`, `ca_5_element_truth_table` (5-prong sweep breaking each element one at a time), and `boundary_29_30_31_day_truth_table` (3-cell precision sweep at 29/30/31 days).

**Florida § 760.27 — personal knowledge + practitioner-jurisdiction test**. Practitioner / telehealth provider must (a) have PERSONAL KNOWLEDGE of the person's disability, (b) act within scope of practice, AND (c) satisfy jurisdiction requirements: telehealth providers must be Florida-licensed; out-of-state practitioners may issue documentation ONLY if they have seen the person IN PERSON at least once. Internet-only registrations, ESA registration cards, vest patches, and similar products are EXPLICITLY UNRELIABLE under § 760.27. Knowingly fraudulent documentation = SECOND-DEGREE MISDEMEANOR under § 760.27(3). Pinned by `fl_all_elements_satisfied_reliable`, `fl_missing_personal_knowledge_unreliable`, `fl_missing_telehealth_compliance_unreliable`, and `fl_internet_only_explicitly_unreliable`.

**Default — Federal FHA + HUD Notice FHEO-2020-01**. Landlord may request reliable documentation if disability or disability-related need is NOT readily apparent. HUD guidance generally disfavors internet-only therapeutic relationships but stops short of categorical rejection. Pinned by `default_licensed_practitioner_non_internet_reliable`, `default_internet_only_unreliable`, `default_unlicensed_unreliable`, and `default_fraudulent_documentation_no_state_misdemeanor` (federal regime has no state misdemeanor).

**Readily-apparent disability short-circuit — applies to all regimes**. Federal FHA + HUD Notice provide that landlord may NOT request documentation when the disability or disability-related need is READILY APPARENT. In this case, no regime's documentation requirements apply; accommodation must be granted absent other lawful basis for denial. Pinned by `readily_apparent_disability_bypasses_documentation` (CA regime, all 5 elements missing but bypassed) and `readily_apparent_applies_to_all_regimes` (3-regime sweep).

**Misdemeanor exposure only in CA and FL** — federal Default regime has no state misdemeanor for fraudulent documentation. Pinned by `ca_fraudulent_documentation_misdemeanor`, `fl_fraudulent_documentation_misdemeanor`, and `misdemeanor_exposure_only_in_ca_and_fl_invariant` (3-regime sweep).

**Multi-regime invariants** — `only_ca_uses_5_element_test_invariant` (3-regime sweep — only CA tests the 5-element practitioner standard); `only_fl_uses_personal_knowledge_test_invariant` (3-regime sweep — only FL tests personal-knowledge); `misdemeanor_exposure_only_in_ca_and_fl_invariant`; `ca_30_day_threshold_constant_invariant` (= 30 days).

**Sibling distinction note** — every result clarifies ESAs are DISTINCT from ADA service animals (covered in `service_animal` module). Service animals are trained for specific disability-related tasks and receive broader protection (ADA Title II/III + FHA + Air Carrier Access Act). ESAs receive housing-only protection. Other related modules: `reasonable_accommodation_modification` (broader FHA framework), `fair_chance_housing` (criminal-background fair housing), `pet_fees` (ESAs are EXEMPT from pet fees under FHA). Pinned by `sibling_distinction_note_present` (UX-text regression).

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/emotional-support-animal-documentation`. Twenty-nine tests pin: **CA all 5 elements satisfied reliable**; **CA missing license unreliable**; **CA missing jurisdiction unreliable**; **CA 29-day relationship unreliable**; **CA exactly 30-day relationship reliable** (boundary); **CA missing evaluation unreliable**; **CA missing warning unreliable**; **CA practitioner discipline on violation**; **FL all elements satisfied reliable**; **FL missing personal knowledge unreliable**; **FL missing telehealth compliance unreliable**; **FL internet-only explicitly unreliable** (§ 760.27 categorical rejection); **default licensed practitioner non-internet reliable**; **default internet-only unreliable**; **default unlicensed unreliable**; **readily-apparent disability bypasses documentation** (short-circuit in CA regime with all 5 elements failed); **readily-apparent applies to all regimes** (3-regime sweep); **CA fraudulent documentation misdemeanor**; **FL fraudulent documentation misdemeanor**; **default fraudulent documentation no state misdemeanor**; **only CA uses 5-element test invariant** (3-regime sweep); **only FL uses personal-knowledge test invariant** (3-regime sweep); **misdemeanor exposure only in CA and FL invariant** (3-regime sweep); **CA 5-element truth table** (5-prong sweep breaking each element); **CA 30-day threshold constant invariant**; **citation pins all authorities** (§ 122318 + AB 468 + Jan 1, 2022 + § 122318(b)/(c) + § 760.27 + § 760.27(2)(b)/(3) + July 1, 2020 + 42 U.S.C. § 3604(f) + HUD Notice FHEO-2020-01 + January 28, 2020 + 24 CFR § 100.204 + ADA § 12102); **sibling distinction note present** (UX-text regression for `service_animal` + `reasonable_accommodation_modification` + `fair_chance_housing` + `pet_fees` + ESA-vs-service-animal + pet-fee exemption); **defensive negative relationship days clamped**; **boundary 29/30/31-day truth table** (3-cell precision sweep).

`traderview-expense::lease_nondisparagement_prohibition` is the **federal Consumer Review Fairness Act (CRFA) lease non-disparagement clause prohibition module** — 15 U.S.C. § 45b (Pub. L. 114-258, December 14, 2016). Distinct from sibling `lease_waiver_enforceability` (general lease waivers under N.Y. GOL § 5-321 + Cal. Civ. Code § 1953 + common law) and `landlord_retaliation_damages` (retaliation against tenant for exercising rights). This module focuses on the NARROW PROHIBITION — landlord-drafted clauses in form (adhesion) lease contracts that gag the tenant from posting honest reviews of landlord/property on Yelp, Google, Apartments.com, Reddit, etc. CRFA creates a federal floor; state attorneys general have concurrent enforcement.

**§ 45b(b) three void-clause categories**:

| Subsection | Provision type | Effect |
|------------|----------------|--------|
| § 45b(b)(1) | Prohibits or restricts review of landlord/property | **Void from inception** |
| § 45b(b)(2) | Imposes penalty or fee for engaging in review | **Void from inception** |
| § 45b(b)(3) | Transfers (or requires transfer of) IP rights in review/feedback content | **Void from inception** |

**§ 45b(a) form-contract gating** — CRFA applies ONLY to form contracts (adhesion contracts with standardized terms imposed without meaningful opportunity to negotiate). Genuinely negotiated leases fall outside CRFA scope; non-disparagement clauses remain subject to common-law contract enforcement under sibling `lease_waiver_enforceability` analysis. Pinned by `non_form_contract_crfa_does_not_apply` (3-clause sweep where CRFA inapplicable even with all 3 voiding clauses present).

**§ 45b(c) four exceptions** (any single exception preserves the challenged clause):

| Subsection | Carve-out |
|------------|-----------|
| § 45b(c)(1) | Legally actionable content (defamation, harassment, knowingly false statements) |
| § 45b(c)(2) | Trade secret or personal information protected under existing law |
| § 45b(c)(3) | Medical-record content (HIPAA) |
| § 45b(c)(4) | Otherwise unlawful content (child pornography, true threats) |

Pinned by individual exception tests for all 4 carve-outs, `exception_blocks_all_three_void_categories` (single exception protects all 3 clause types), and `exception_truth_table_for_4_carve_outs` (**16-cell sweep** — any single exception true triggers engagement; only all-false leaves clause void).

**§ 45b(d) offering prohibition — independent violation**. Offering a form contract containing a void provision is ITSELF unlawful, separate from any attempt at enforcement. Landlord exposed to FTC enforcement under § 5 of FTC Act (15 U.S.C. § 45, unfair or deceptive acts) and state attorney general civil action. Pinned by `offering_violation_independent_of_enforcement_attempt`.

**§ 45b(e) concurrent enforcement** — FTC enforces under § 5 of FTC Act (unfair or deceptive act). State attorneys general have CONCURRENT enforcement authority; may bring federal-district-court civil action for injunctive relief, damages, restitution, civil penalties, or other equitable relief. FTC Bulletin 2022-05 (March 2022) provides interpretive guidance.

**Three-clause truth table** — `three_clause_truth_table` (**8-cell sweep** over non-disparagement × penalty × IP-transfer; void count matches sum of present-clauses-without-exception). Pinned by `all_three_clauses_void_simultaneously`.

**Sibling distinction note** — every result references `lease_waiver_enforceability` (general lease waivers), `landlord_retaliation_damages` (retaliation for tenant review may be parallel cause of action), and `landlord_harassment`, clarifying CRFA is a federal FLOOR — state-law remedies remain available and may provide additional damages. Pinned by `sibling_distinction_note_present`.

**Enforcement note** — every result references FTC + State attorneys general concurrent enforcement + FTC Bulletin 2022-05. Pinned by `ftc_state_ag_enforcement_note_present` (UX-text regression).

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/lease-nondisparagement-prohibition`. Twenty-two tests pin: **non-form contract CRFA does not apply** (3-clause sweep inapplicable); **form contract no voiding clauses compliant** (baseline); **non-disparagement clause void no exception** (§ 45b(b)(1)); **penalty clause void no exception** (§ 45b(b)(2)); **IP transfer clause void no exception** (§ 45b(b)(3)); **legally actionable content exception preserves clause** (§ 45b(c)(1)); **trade secret exception preserves clause** (§ 45b(c)(2)); **medical record exception preserves clause** (§ 45b(c)(3)); **unlawful content exception preserves clause** (§ 45b(c)(4)); **exception blocks all three void categories** (single exception covers); **all three clauses void simultaneously**; **offering violation independent of enforcement attempt** (§ 45b(d)); **no void clause no offering violation**; **FTC and state AG enforcement available on voiding**; **no enforcement available on compliant lease**; **three-clause truth table** (8-cell sweep over non-disparagement × penalty × IP-transfer); **exception truth table for 4 carve-outs** (16-cell exhaustive sweep); **citation pins all subsections** (15 U.S.C. § 45b + Pub. L. 114-258 + Dec 14, 2016 + § 45b(a)/(b)(1)/(b)(2)/(b)(3)/(c)/(d)/(e) + § 45 FTC Act + FTC Bulletin 2022-05); **sibling distinction note present** (UX-text regression for `lease_waiver_enforceability` + `landlord_retaliation_damages` + `landlord_harassment` + federal-floor concept); **FTC + state AG enforcement note present** (UX-text regression for concurrent enforcement + Bulletin 2022-05); **lease with no offending provisions compliant**; **lease with exception only carve-out compliant**.

`traderview-expense::plain_language_lease` is the **state plain-language lease / consumer-contract requirement compliance table** — only 4 states have enacted statutes specifically requiring residential leases to be written in clear, readable language understandable to the average tenant. The 1970s consumer-protection movement produced the original wave: CT (1979, U.S. pioneer), NY (1978-effective), NJ (1981), PA (1993). The 46 other states + DC rely on common-law unconscionability + contract-of-adhesion doctrines.

**Five regimes** with sharply different remedies:

| Regime | States | Standard | Statutory penalty | Defenses |
|--------|--------|----------|--------------------|----------|
| `NewYorkClearCoherent50DollarPenalty` | NY | "Clear and coherent" + "common and everyday meanings" + captioned sections | **$50** + actual damages | Good-faith compliance attempt; **barred after both parties fully performed** |
| `NewJersey100DollarMinimumPlusAttorneyFees` | NJ | "Simple, clear, understandable and easily readable" | Greater of **$100 minimum** OR actual damages + **reasonable attorney fees** | (none) |
| `PennsylvaniaPlainLanguageNineTests` | PA | Substantial compliance with **9 objective tests** (short sentences + everyday words + personal pronouns + simple/active verbs + readable type + ink contrast + boldface captions + layout/spacing + clear/coherent organization) | Private remedy + attorney fees | AG preapproval procedure |
| `ConnecticutDescriptiveReadability` | CT | Hybrid: descriptive standard + objective readability score (Flesch Reading Ease) | Private remedy + attorney fees | (none) |
| `NoStatewidePlainLanguageRequirement` | 46 other states + DC | (none) | (none) | Common-law unconscionability only |

**NY uniquely combines two defenses**: GOL § 5-702 gives the landlord both a good-faith-compliance defense AND a hard bar on tenant actions after both parties have fully performed. NJ has neither.

**PA's 9-test substantial-compliance regime is the most objective**: 8-of-9 tests passed still fails. The statute requires "substantial" compliance with the full list.

**NY effective date gate**: only leases executed on or after 1978-11-01 are covered. Pre-1978 lease forms still in use are grandfathered out.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/plain-language-lease-check`. Thirty-three tests pin: **5 regime classifications** (NY / NJ / PA / CT + default AL/CA/FL/TX/WA/MA/DC/WY); **NY compliant lease no penalty**; **NY non-compliant lease → actual $500 + $50 penalty = $550**; **NY good-faith defense applies** (zeros penalty); **NY full-performance barred** (zeros penalty); **NY pre-1978 lease statute does not apply**; **NJ actual $50 below $100 → $100 minimum applies**; **NJ actual $5,000 → uses actual**; **NJ attorney fees recoverable on violation**; NJ no attorney fees when compliant; **NJ has NO good-faith defense path** (regression — only NY does); **PA all 9 tests passed + lease complies → compliant**; **PA 8-of-9 tests satisfied → non-compliant** (regression target — substantial compliance requires all 9); PA attorney fees recoverable; default state no statute applies; **4 citation regression targets** (NY § 5-702 + "$50 statutory penalty" + "good-faith"; NJ 56:12-1 + "$100 statutory minimum" + "attorney fees"; PA 73 P.S. § 2201 + "9 objective tests"; CT § 42-152 + "oldest"); **51-state coverage**; non-empty citations; **5 single-state-uniqueness invariants** (NY-only 50-dollar / NJ-only 100-minimum / PA-only 9-tests / CT-only descriptive-readability / **NY-only good-faith defense**); NY good-faith note mentions defense; NY full-performance note mentions "statutorily barred"; lowercase state code normalizes.

`traderview-expense::roommate_authorization` is the **state tenant roommate / additional-occupant authorization compliance table** — distinct from `sublet_consent` (which is assignment / re-leasing of the entire unit) and from `occupancy_standards` (building-code limits). This module captures the tenant's STATUTORY right to bring in an unrelated adult occupant despite contrary lease terms. Only 2 states create such a right.

**Three regimes**:

| Regime | States | Mechanism | Lease restrictions |
|--------|--------|-----------|---------------------|
| `NewYorkStatutoryRoommateRight` | NY | RPL § 235-f Roommate Law — 1 additional adult occupant per tenant + immediate family + occupant's dependent children + tenant's primary residence + 30-day landlord notification | **VOID and unenforceable as against public policy** under § 235-f(7) |
| `CaliforniaTwoPlusOneFormula` | CA | State-law occupancy formula: 2 per bedroom + 1 additional | Enforceable above the statutory floor |
| `DefaultLeaseGoverns` | 48 other states + DC | Lease terms control | Enforceable |

**NY RPL § 235-f is uniquely powerful**: lease prohibitions on unrelated roommates are statutorily VOID. The tenant's right does NOT depend on landlord consent. Multi-tenant lease cap: total tenants + occupants ≤ number of tenants on the lease (so a 2-tenant lease permits 2 additional adult occupants total — one per tenant).

**CA "2 plus 1" arithmetic**:
- 1-bedroom unit → max 3 occupants
- 2-bedroom unit → max 5 occupants
- 3-bedroom unit → max 7 occupants
- N-bedroom unit → max 2N + 1 occupants

**NY 30-day notification window**: tenant must inform the landlord of the new occupant's name within 30 days of occupancy commencement OR within 30 days of the landlord's request, whichever is later. Failure to notify does not void the right but creates a separate lease-compliance issue.

**NJ de-facto co-tenancy doctrine** (default regime): tenants in default-regime NJ can argue de-facto co-tenancy when an unauthorized roommate has shown continuous residence + substantial financial contribution + landlord acknowledgment. Not modeled here as a regime since it's a judicial doctrine, not a statutory right.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/roommate-authorization-check`. Twenty-eight tests pin: **3 regime classifications** (NY / CA + default AL/FL/TX/WA/DC/WY/MA/NJ/IL); **NY single-tenant 1 additional adult permitted**; **NY 2 additional adults exceeds per-tenant cap of 1** (regression — RPL § 235-f permits only 1 per tenant); **NY not-primary-residence → no statutory right**; **NY lease restriction VOID when statute permits** (regression target — only NY does this); **NY 30-day notification window** (day 30 within; day 31 without notification non-compliant); **NY multi-tenant 2-on-lease + 2 additional = OK** (cap 2 tenants × 1 additional each = 2); **NY multi-tenant 2-on-lease + 3 additional exceeds cap**; **CA 1-bedroom max 3 occupants** ($N \times 2 + 1 = 3$); **CA 1-bedroom 4 occupants exceeds max**; **CA 2-bedroom max 5**; **CA 3-bedroom max 7**; **CA lease remains enforceable above floor** (regression — CA does NOT void lease restrictions); default-state no-lease-restriction permitted; default-state lease-restriction blocks roommate; **2 citation regression targets** (NY § 235-f + "VOID" + "30 days"; CA "2 plus 1" + "2 per bedroom"); **51-state coverage**; non-empty citations; **3 single-state-uniqueness invariants** (NY-only / CA-only / **NY-only lease-restrictions-void**); NY note describes per-tenant cap arithmetic; CA note describes bedroom formula; lowercase state code normalizes.

`traderview-expense::otard_antenna_installation` is the **federal FCC Over-the-Air Reception Devices (OTARD) rule compliance check for tenant antenna / satellite-dish / fixed-wireless-receiver installation** — sibling to `ev_charger_installation` (tenant right-to-install electric vehicle charging). 47 CFR § 1.4000 **PREEMPTS** state, local, HOA, and lease restrictions that impair a tenant's ability to install, maintain, or use a covered antenna in an area within the tenant's exclusive use or control. Trader-relevant for traders who depend on satellite-TV market data feeds, broadcast-TV business news (CNBC / Bloomberg over-the-air), or fixed-wireless broadband for low-latency price feeds.

**Five protected antenna types under § 1.4000(a)(1)**:

| Type | Size limit | Notes |
|------|------------|-------|
| `DBS` | ≤ 1 meter diameter | Direct broadcast satellite (DirecTV, Dish Network); no size limit in Alaska |
| `MMDS` | ≤ 1 meter diameter or diagonal | Multipoint distribution service / wireless cable |
| `BroadcastTV` | **Any size** | Over-the-air TV receive antenna |
| Supporting mast | (none stated) | Mast supporting any of the above |
| `FixedWirelessHubRelay2021` | (2021 expansion) | FCC 21-10 R&O eff. 2021-03-29 — requires on-premises customer AND broadband-only |

**Three permissible restriction categories under § 1.4000(b)**:

| Category | Statute | Subject to no-impairment test |
|----------|---------|--------------------------------|
| `Safety` | § 1.4000(b)(1) | YES — must not unreasonably delay/cost/signal |
| `HistoricPreservation` | § 1.4000(b)(2) | YES — National Register properties only |
| `Aesthetic` | NEVER permissible | n/a |
| `BlanketProhibition` | NEVER permissible | n/a — "no antennas of any kind" clauses are void |
| `PreApprovalDelay` | NEVER permissible | n/a — § 1.4000(a)(3)(i) |

**§ 1.4000(a)(3) no-impairment standard** has three independent failure modes — any one defeats an otherwise-permissible safety or historic-preservation restriction:
- Unreasonably **delays** or prevents installation
- Unreasonably **increases the cost** of installation, maintenance, or use
- **Precludes** reception or transmission of an acceptable-quality signal

**The 2021 OTARD expansion (FCC 21-10) is the trader-critical addition.** Fixed-wireless hub/relay antennas — the kind that brings high-speed broadband to apartment buildings via roof-mounted distribution antennas — are protected ONLY IF (a) the antenna serves a customer on whose premises it is located, AND (b) the service provided is broadband-only. Both requirements must be satisfied; failure of either drops the antenna out of OTARD scope. Pinned by `fixed_wireless_2021_with_both_requirements_protected`, `fixed_wireless_2021_without_on_premises_customer_not_protected`, `fixed_wireless_2021_without_broadband_only_not_protected`, and the invariant `fixed_wireless_2021_expansion_thresholds_required_invariant` (4-cell truth table: only (true, true) protects).

**Tenant-exclusive-use scope is the location-side gate.** Patios, balconies, single-tenant rooftops, owned condo unit interior facing exterior all qualify. Common areas (shared rooftops, exterior walls of apartment buildings, hallways, shared yards) are OUTSIDE the rule's scope — landlord retains restriction authority on those. Pinned by `common_area_installation_not_protected` and `common_area_with_protected_antenna_type_still_unprotected` (confirms location overrides antenna-type protection across all 4 covered types).

**§ 1.4000(c) burden of proof.** When OTARD applies, the burden of demonstrating that a particular restriction complies with the rule rests on the party seeking to impose or maintain the restriction (the landlord / HOA). Enforcement of conflicting restrictions is stayed pending FCC or court review. Pinned by `burden_on_restricting_party_when_otard_protected` and `no_burden_when_otard_not_protected`.

**Aesthetic and blanket-prohibition restrictions are NEVER permissible regardless of antenna type.** § 1.4000 does not list aesthetic concerns as a permissible restriction category. Lease clauses that say "no antennas of any kind" or "satellite dishes prohibited" are void as preempted. Pinned by `aesthetic_restriction_never_permissible`, `blanket_prohibition_never_permissible`, `pre_approval_delay_not_permissible`, and the invariant `aesthetic_blanket_pre_approval_invalidate_regardless_of_antenna_type` (4 antenna types × 3 restriction types = 12 confirmed-invalid cells).

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/otard-antenna-installation`. Twenty-six tests pin: **DBS on patio protected**; **MMDS on balcony protected**; **broadcast TV any size protected**; **antenna outside OTARD scope not protected**; **fixed-wireless 2021 with both requirements protected**; **without on-premises customer not protected**; **without broadband-only not protected**; **common area installation not protected**; **common area with protected antenna type still unprotected** (location overrides type); **safety restriction without impairment permissible**; **safety with unreasonable delay not permissible**; **safety with cost increase not permissible**; **safety with signal preclusion not permissible**; **historic preservation without impairment permissible**; **historic with impairment not permissible**; **aesthetic never permissible**; **blanket prohibition never permissible**; **pre-approval delay not permissible**; **burden on restricting party when OTARD protected**; **no burden when not protected**; **aesthetic + blanket + pre-approval invalidate regardless of antenna type 12-cell invariant**; **safety + historic subject to no-impairment standard 6-cell invariant** (2 restriction types × 3 impairment flags); **citation pins 2021 expansion for fixed wireless**; **preemption note across all 10 paths** (5 antenna × 2 location); **fixed-wireless 2021 expansion thresholds required 4-cell invariant**; **no restriction path compliant when protected**.

`traderview-expense::tenant_solar_installation` is the **state tenant right-to-install solar-energy-system compliance check** — emerging area parallel to `ev_charger_installation` (tenant right-to-charge EV). Most state solar-rights laws cover homeowners + HOAs + condos but not rental tenants directly; the push to extend protection to renters is recent (2024-2026), with portable / plug-in solar systems leading the way because they require no permanent installation.

**Four regimes**:

| Regime | Authority | Statutory tenant protection |
|--------|-----------|------------------------------|
| `California` | Cal. Civ. Code § 714 (Solar Rights Act) + § 714.1 + § 4600 + § 4746 | Plug-in portable + roof-mounted (with exclusive-use) |
| `Colorado` | Colorado HB22-1020 + 2026 plug-in solar legalization bill | Plug-in portable only |
| `NewJersey` | N.J.S.A. 45:22A-48.2 (Planned Real Estate Development Full Disclosure Act) | None for rental tenants (HOA-focused) |
| `Default` | Various state solar-rights laws | None for rental tenants |

**Three installation types**:

| Type | Permissibility | Common protection |
|------|----------------|---------------------|
| `PlugInPortable` | Most permissive (balcony / window mount) | CA + CO |
| `RoofMounted` | Typically requires consent | CA only (with exclusive use) |
| `GroundMounted` | Always requires consent | None |

**California § 714 Solar Rights Act renders restrictions on solar energy systems "void and unenforceable."** § 714 applies most directly to plug-in portable installations. Roof-mounted installations require the tenant to have exclusive use or control of the installation area (similar to OTARD's tenant-exclusive-use scope). Pinned by `california_plug_in_portable_statutorily_protected`, `california_roof_mounted_with_exclusive_use_protected`, `california_roof_mounted_without_exclusive_use_not_protected`, and the 4-regime invariant `only_california_protects_roof_mounted_with_exclusive_use_invariant`.

**Colorado 2026 plug-in solar legalization** establishes regulatory framework for portable arrays without landlord prohibition. Builds on HB22-1020 (Customer Right To Use Energy 2022). Pinned by `colorado_plug_in_portable_protected_for_renters`, `colorado_roof_mounted_not_statutorily_protected_for_renters`, `colorado_ground_mounted_not_protected`.

**Universal safety thresholds apply across all regimes**:
- Installation must meet electrical / safety code
- Installation must not damage landlord property (or restoration agreement in place)

Failure of either threshold prevents installation regardless of regime or installation type. Pinned by `unsafe_installation_never_permitted_invariant` (12-cell: 4 regimes × 3 installation types) and `property_damage_never_permitted_invariant` (4 regimes).

**Ground-mounted yard installation is never statutorily protected.** Across all four regimes, ground-mounted requires landlord consent. Pinned by `ground_mounted_never_statutorily_protected_invariant`.

**Installation-permitted truth table** = `safety_ok AND (statutory_protection OR landlord_consent)`. 8-cell truth table pinned by `installation_permitted_iff_safety_and_either_protected_or_consented_invariant`.

**Plug-in portable protection is unique to CA + CO.** Of the four regimes, only California (via § 714 Solar Rights Act) and Colorado (via HB22-1020 + 2026 bill) statutorily protect renter installation of portable solar. NJ + Default require landlord consent. Pinned by `only_california_and_colorado_protect_plug_in_portable_invariant` (4-regime invariant).

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/tenant-solar-installation`. Nineteen tests pin: **CA plug-in portable statutorily protected + roof-mounted with exclusive use protected + roof-mounted without exclusive use not protected + ground-mounted requires consent**; **CO plug-in portable protected for renters + roof-mounted not protected + ground-mounted not protected**; **NJ HOA focus no tenant rental protection**; **Default no tenant rental protection**; **unsafe installation never permitted 12-cell invariant** (4 regimes × 3 installation types); **property damage never permitted 4-regime invariant**; **landlord consent permits otherwise unprotected installation**; **missing consent violation when not statutorily protected**; **only-California-and-Colorado-protect-plug-in-portable 4-regime invariant**; **only-California-protects-roof-mounted-with-exclusive-use 4-regime invariant**; **ground-mounted never statutorily protected 4-regime invariant**; **installation permitted iff safety AND (statutory or consent) 8-cell truth-table invariant**; **citation pins authority per regime** (§ 714 for CA; HB22-1020 for CO; § 45:22A-48.2 for NJ; "Most states" for Default); **sibling-module note across all 12 combos** (UX-text regression for ev_charger_installation pairing).

`traderview-expense::ev_charger_installation` is the **state tenant right-to-install electric-vehicle charging station compliance table** — modern "right-to-charge" laws addressing demand for at-home EV charging in multi-unit rental housing. Only 4 states grant tenant-specific protections; MD / VA / CO / FL right-to-charge laws cover HOAs and condos but do not extend to standalone rental tenants.

**Five regimes** with sharply different gates:

| Regime | States | Insurance | Building gate | Lease restrictions |
|--------|--------|-----------|----------------|--------------------|
| `CaliforniaInsuranceRequired` | CA | **$1,000,000 general liability** | None | Enforceable above 4-prong requirements |
| `HawaiiLeaseProvisionVoid` | HI | None | Multi-family residential or townhouse | **VOID and unenforceable** under HRS § 196-7.5 |
| `IllinoisNewBuildingsOnly` | IL | None | **NEW construction only** + multi-unit | Reasonable-terms review |
| `NewJerseyMultiUnitRight` | NJ | None | Multi-unit residential | Reasonable-terms review |
| `DefaultLeaseGoverns` | 46 other states + DC | None | None | Enforceable |

**CA 4-prong test** — ALL required for tenant eligibility:
1. Lease signed / renewed / extended on/after 2015-07-01
2. Tenant willing to pay charging-station + installation + utility costs
3. Tenant signs written agreement covering installation, use, maintenance, removal
4. Tenant maintains **$1,000,000 general-liability insurance** policy

**HI is the strongest tenant-protective regime**: any lease provision purporting to restrict EV charger installation is VOID and unenforceable. Protection extends beyond dedicated parking stalls to common-element parking (first-come, first-served).

**IL applies only to new construction**: the Electric Vehicle Charging Act mandates 100% of parking spaces at new multi-unit dwellings be EV-ready (conduit + reserved capacity). Tenants in older buildings remain lease-governed even in IL.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/ev-charger-installation-check`. Thirty-one tests pin: **5 regime classifications** (CA / HI / IL / NJ + default AL/FL/TX/WA/DC/WY/NY/MA); **CA all 4 prongs met → eligible** (lease date + pay-costs + written request + agreement + $1M insurance); **CA insurance below $1M not eligible** ($500k → fails); **CA insurance exactly $1M eligible**; CA no-insurance-carried not eligible; CA tenant-unwilling-to-pay not eligible; CA no-written-request not eligible; CA no-written-agreement not eligible; **CA lease pre-effective-date statute inapplicable** (regression — only post-2015-07-01 leases covered); **HI baseline eligible with NO insurance required** (regression — HI does NOT require $1M); **HI lease restriction VOID when statute permits** (lease unenforceable); **IL new construction eligible**; **IL older construction statute inapplicable** (regression — IL only covers NEW buildings); IL not-multi-unit statute inapplicable; **NJ multi-unit eligible**; NJ single-family inapplicable; default state no-lease-restriction eligible; default state lease-restriction blocks install; **3 citation regression targets** (CA § 1947.6 + "$1,000,000"; HI § 196-7.5 + "VOID"; IL 765 ILCS + "NEW"); **51-state coverage**; non-empty citations; **3 single-state-uniqueness invariants** (CA-only insurance-required / HI-only lease-void / IL-only new-construction); lowercase state code normalizes.

`traderview-expense::advance_rent_limit` is the **state tenant advance-rent prepayment limit compliance table** — distinct from `security_deposit_caps` (which caps refundable deposits). Trader-tenants relocating to expensive markets (NYC, SF, Boston) routinely face landlord demands for 6-12 months prepaid rent; only 4 states cap or prohibit these.

**Five regimes** with sharply different remedies:

| Regime | States | Maximum months | Interest required | 6-month gate |
|--------|--------|----------------|--------------------|---------------|
| `NewYorkOneMonthFirstOnly` | NY | **1 month** (first only) | None | None |
| `MassachusettsFirstLastSecurityLock` | MA | 2 months (first + last) | **5% annual on last-month** | None |
| `CaliforniaSixMonthLeaseOnly` | CA | 1 month default; **6+ months only when lease ≥ 6 months covering ≥ 6 months** | None | **Yes** |
| `NewJerseyAdvanceRentUnlimited` | NJ | (unlimited — not subject to 1.5-month security deposit cap) | None | None |
| `NoStateAdvanceRentLimit` | 46 other states + DC | (unlimited) | None | None |

**NY HSTPA 2019 is the strictest regime**: RPL § 238-a prohibits demanding last month's rent or multi-month prepayments as a condition of move-in. Combined with the 1-month security deposit cap, NYC landlords are statutorily limited to **first month + 1 month security = 2 months total** at signing. This applies ONLY to unregulated tenancies — rent-stabilized units retain their pre-HSTPA practices.

**MA's unique 5% interest requirement**: G.L. c. 186 § 15B requires landlords who collect advance last-month rent to pay 5% annual interest (or the lower bank-deposit rate). No other state in this table requires interest on advance rent.

**CA's 6-month gate** (Civ. Code § 1950.5): tenants may prepay multiple months ONLY when (i) the lease term is at least 6 months AND (ii) the prepaid amount covers at least 6 months. This bars landlords from pressuring month-to-month tenants into multi-month prepayments — a common Bay Area practice before 1950.5 amendments.

**NJ's interpretive clarification** (N.J.S.A. 46:8-21.2): advance rent is statutorily NOT a security deposit and therefore NOT subject to the 1.5-month security-deposit cap under § 46:8-19. NJ is effectively a no-cap state on advance rent specifically.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/advance-rent-limit-check`. Thirty-one tests pin: **5 regime classifications** (NY / MA / CA / NJ + default AL/FL/TX/WA/DC/WY/IL); **NY one-month demand compliant**; **NY first + last violates cap** (regression — HSTPA prohibits last-month demand); NY 3 months violates cap; **NY regulated tenancy does NOT apply HSTPA cap** (regression — only unregulated covered); **MA first only compliant**; **MA first + last + 5% interest compliant**; **MA first + last WITHOUT 5% interest non-compliant** (regression — distinguishing MA from NJ); MA 3 months violates cap; **CA 1-month demand any-lease-term compliant**; **CA 6-month advance with 6-month lease compliant**; **CA 6-month advance with 5-month lease non-compliant** (regression — strict ≥ 6 lease months); **CA 3-month advance even with long lease non-compliant** (regression — must be ≥ 6 months to use the multi-month path); **NJ unlimited 12-month demand compliant** (no cap); default state any amount compliant; **4 citation regression targets** (NY § 238-a + HSTPA + "PROHIBITED"; MA c. 186 § 15B + "5% annual interest"; CA § 1950.5 + "≥ 6 months"; NJ 46:8-21.2); **51-state coverage**; non-empty citations; **3 single-state-uniqueness invariants** (NY-only 1-month / MA-only 5%-interest / CA-only 6-month-gate); NY violation note mentions "advance-rent cap violated"; MA interest-violation note mentions "5% interest"; lowercase state code normalizes.

`traderview-expense::fire_sprinkler_disclosure` is the **state fire-sprinkler disclosure requirement compliance table** — distinct from `detector_requirements` (smoke/CO installation) and `heat_requirements` (heating adequacy). Captures the statutory requirement that residential leases contain a conspicuous notice about the presence/absence of a fire-sprinkler system in the leased premises. Only New York has a specific statutory disclosure rule; other states rely on building/fire codes that do NOT extend to lease-text disclosure.

**Two regimes**:

| Regime | States | Statutory mandate | Bold-face required | Maintenance date required |
|--------|--------|--------------------|---------------------|----------------------------|
| `NewYorkSprinklerSystemNotice` | NY | Yes (RPL § 231-a eff. 2014-12-03) | **Yes** (bold face type) | **Yes** when system present (last maintenance and inspection date) |
| `NoStateFireSprinklerDisclosureLaw` | 49 other states + DC | No statewide rule | (n/a) | (n/a) |

**NY § 231-a has NO statutory penalty provision** — the effect of noncompliance is uncertain until tested in litigation. In practice, courts may construe the lease against the landlord (contra proferentem) and could find the lease unenforceable as to fire-related issues. The lease-rider remedy (rather than fixed-dollar penalty) is what distinguishes NY § 231-a from other tenant-protection statutes like `plain_language_lease` (NY § 5-702's $50 fixed penalty) and `lease_copy_delivery` (MA c. 186 § 15D's $300 fixed fine).

**Conditional disclosure structure**: the maintenance-date requirement attaches ONLY when the sprinkler system actually exists. A unit with NO sprinkler system needs only the "no sprinkler system" disclosure — no maintenance-date prong applies.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/fire-sprinkler-disclosure-check`. Eighteen tests pin: **2 regime classifications** (NY + default AL/CA/FL/TX/WA/DC/WY/MA/NJ/IL); **NY all 4 compliance facets met** (notice in lease + bold face + maintenance date + system present); **NY no notice non-compliant** with diagnostic; **NY notice NOT bold face non-compliant** (regression target — bold-face prong independently checked); **NY no maintenance date non-compliant when system present**; **NY no sprinkler system → no maintenance-date requirement** (regression — conditional structure); default state no-disclosure-required; default state no-notice still compliant (no obligation); **2 citation regression targets** (NY § 231-a + "BOLD FACE TYPE" + 2014-12-03; "NO statutory penalty" pin); **51-state coverage**; non-empty citations; **2 single-state-uniqueness invariants** (NY-only sprinkler-notice / NY-only bold-face-required); NY compliant note mentions regime; default state note says "no statutory disclosure obligation"; lowercase state code normalizes.

`traderview-expense::carpet_replacement_useful_life` is the **multi-state carpet-replacement useful-life landlord security-deposit deduction compliance check** — addresses the most heavily litigated security-deposit dispute: landlord charges tenant for carpet replacement at move-out; tenant argues the carpet was already old and the charge exceeds the landlord's actual loss. The doctrine has crystallized into per-state "useful life" schedules — landlord may deduct only the **remaining-life fraction** of replacement cost, never the full amount.

**Four regimes**:

| Regime | Authority | Useful life | Special damage gate | Cost-recovery formula |
|--------|-----------|-------------|----------------------|------------------------|
| `ColoradoHb251249` | Colo. Rev. Stat. § 38-12-104 (HB 25-1249 eff. 2026-01-01) | **10 years** | **Substantial + irreparable damage AND replaced new within 10 years** | Minimum amount necessary, prorated |
| `California` | Common law — Killough v. McManus 19 Cal. App. 3d 1141 (1971) + progeny | **8 years** | None (normal wear-and-tear analysis only) | Prorated remaining-life fraction |
| `HudSection8` | HUD Handbook 4350.3 chap. 6 | **7 years** | None (HUD schedule) | Prorated |
| `Default` | Common law actual-damages-net-of-depreciation | 7 years (proxy) | None | Prorated |

**Colorado's 10-year useful life is the longest** (HB 25-1249 extended from 7 years to 10 effective 2026-01-01). Colorado also adds two unique gate requirements: (a) substantial + irreparable damage exceeding normal wear-and-tear AND (b) carpet replaced new within the 10-year window preceding lease termination. Failing EITHER gate → $0 deduction allowed. Other states use straight remaining-life proration without these gates.

**Section 8 override**: HUD Section 8 voucher units use the federal HUD schedule (7-year) regardless of state — Section 8 status overrides state routing.

**Past useful life = $0 deduction across all regimes**: when carpet age ≥ useful life, the carpet is fully depreciated and landlord cannot charge for replacement. The 10-year threshold for CO + 8-year for CA + 7-year for HUD/Default are the per-state expiration points.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/carpet-replacement-useful-life`. Twenty-two tests pin: **CO past 10 years zero deduction** + 10-year useful life; **CO at 10-year boundary past**; **CO 9 years with damage 1 year remaining proration** ($2000 × 1/10 = $200); **CO no-substantial-damage no deduction** (regression-critical Colorado-only gate); **CO not-replaced-new-within-10-years no deduction** (regression-critical second gate); **CA past 8 years zero deduction**; **CA at 8-year boundary past**; **CA 4-year proration half** ($2000 × 4/8 = $1000); **CA no-damage-gate proration still applies** (regression — CA does NOT have CO's substantial+irreparable gate); **HUD § 8 carpet at 7 years past** + 7-year useful life; **HUD § 8 3-year proration** ($700 × 4/7 = $400); **default 7-year useful life**; **default past 7 years zero**; **negative cost clamped**; **state routing CO/CA/Default + Section 8 override** (regression — Section 8 voucher overrides state routing regardless of CO/CA); **CO useful life LONGEST at 10 years** (CO 10 > CA 8 > HUD 7 = Default 7 ordering invariant); **only-CO-has-substantial-damage-gate** 4-regime invariant (regression-critical — same no-damage scenario yields zero for CO + prorated for others); **only-CO-requires-replaced-new-within-useful-life** 2-regime invariant; **citations pin authorities** (HB 25-1249 + 2026-01-01 + Killough + HUD Handbook 4350.3 + common-law); **CA brand-new carpet full replacement cost minus proration** ($2000 fully recoverable at 0 years age); **CO with damage and recent replacement proration applies** (gates open → standard proration kicks in: $2000 × 5/10 = $1000).

`traderview-expense::pesticide_application_notice` is the **state landlord pesticide-application notice compliance check** — operational concern for any landlord using a pest-control company or self-applying pesticides. Sibling to `bedbug_extermination_cost` (treatment-responsibility) and `bedbug_disclosure` (initial-tenancy disclosure). Federal **FIFRA** (7 U.S.C. § 136 et seq.) imposes labeling + applicator-licensing requirements but does NOT require advance notice to occupants — that is purely state law, and the states diverge sharply.

**Six regimes**:

| Regime | Authority | Tenant advance notice | Adjacent unit notice | Building posting | Unique content requirement |
|--------|-----------|------------------------|------------------------|-------------------|-----------------------------|
| `California` | Cal. Civ. Code § 1940.8.5 + 14 CCR § 6740 | **24 hours** | **Yes** for broadcast / fogger / aerosol | No | Pesticide product + manufacturer + EPA registration + applicator name |
| `NewJersey` | N.J.A.C. § 7:30-9.12 | At-application only | No | No | Applicator contact + **National Pesticide Information Center phone** + NJDEP Pesticide Control Program phone |
| `NewYork` | N.Y. ECL § 33-1004 | None (on request only for multi-dwelling) | No | No | Label info via applicator; multi-dwelling owner relays on request |
| `Massachusetts` | Mass. G.L. c. 132B § 9 + 333 CMR 13.04 | **48 hours** (strictest in matrix) | No | **Yes** at building entrances | Integrated pest management framework |
| `Oregon` | ORS § 634.740 | None to tenant | No | **24 hours** "Warning: pesticide-treated area" sign + 72-hour minimum retention | Date/time + contact phone |
| `Default` | FIFRA 7 U.S.C. § 136 et seq. | None statewide | No | No | Federal labeling floor only |

**California's adjacent-unit notice requirement is the unique state expansion.** Cal. Civ. Code § 1940.8.5 extends notice to adjacent dwelling units that could "reasonably be impacted" — but ONLY for broadcast applications, total release foggers, and aerosol sprays. Targeted/spot applications get notice only to the treated unit. Pinned by `california_broadcast_requires_adjacent_unit_notice`, `california_fogger_requires_adjacent_unit_notice`, `california_aerosol_requires_adjacent_unit_notice`, `california_broadcast_missing_adjacent_notice_violation`, and the 6-regime invariant `only_california_requires_adjacent_unit_notice_invariant` (confirmed 5 other regimes do NOT extend to adjacent units even for broadcast applications).

**Massachusetts has the strictest advance-notice window — 48 hours.** Twice California's 24-hour mandate; double the strictest other regime. Pinned by `massachusetts_under_48h_violation`, `massachusetts_at_48h_boundary_compliant`, `massachusetts_missing_building_posting_violation`, and the comparative invariant `massachusetts_has_strictest_advance_notice_window` (MA > CA, MA > OR, MA > NJ, MA > NY).

**New Jersey's National Pesticide Information Center phone is the unique state-specific notice content.** N.J.A.C. § 7:30-9.12 requires the NPIC phone number be included in the at-application label-information delivery. No other regime in this matrix mandates it. Pinned by `new_jersey_missing_npic_phone_violation` and the invariant `only_nj_requires_npic_phone_invariant` (5 other regimes do NOT cite NPIC phone absence as a violation).

**Massachusetts and Oregon are the only building-posting jurisdictions.** MA requires 48-hour posting at building entrances alongside the 48-hour tenant notice; OR requires 24-hour sign posting around the application area but no direct tenant-notice mandate.
